Systems showing extended star formation histories (Carina and Leo I) however, are consistent with the idea that their tidally limited dark haloes provide the necessary gravitational potential wells to retain their gas. The complex time structure of the star formation in these systems, remains difficult to understand. Observations of detailed abundance ratios for Ursa Minor, strongly suggest that the star formation history of this galaxy might in fact resemble the complex picture presented by Carina or Leo I, but localized at a very early epoch.

sources.summary: The paper says UMi contains a low-velocity substructure whose survival requires weak central tides and a cored mass profile; MOND still requires high mass-to-light or a different scale parameter.

quotes.quote: In UMi, an otherwise very simple system from an astrophysical perspective, an extremely low velocity dispersion sub-structure exists (figure 4a).

The group of stars have the same mean velocity as the systemic velocity of UMi, so they must orbit close to the plane of the sky, and hence through the central regions of UMi. As figure (4b) illustrates, this is possible only if the tidal forces from the UMi central mass gradient are weak. In fact, survival of this phase-space structure in configuration space requires that UMi has a cored mass profile.
For UMi and Draco, however, even MOND requires either a high M/L value ( 20 ), or a very different length scale parameter ( a_0 50 10^ 8 cm s ^ 2 ).

sources.summary: The paper identifies Ursa Minor as the strongest dwarf-spheroidal gamma-ray target, gives expected flux ratios, and estimates detectable neutralino-annihilation flux within a 0.1 degree aperture.

quotes.quote: Ursa Minor is the most promising dSph candidate for detection and we present the expected ray flux ratios between the remaining five dSphs and Ursa Minor.
hbtp Figure fig:fluxfigure shows that the most promising candidates for detection are Ursa Minor Draco, with the largest flux coming from Ursa Minor.

Assuming a smooth dark matter distribution in the dSph halos, we find that Ursa Minor and Draco are the most promising dSph's for detecting products of dark matter annihilation. Fornax and Sculptor are a factor of 10 fainter, while Carina and Sextans are fainter by a factor of 100 . In the most optimistic scenario for neutralino dark matter, the largest-predicted flux from Ursa Minor is 3 10^ 11 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 . This is the flux within a 0.1 degree radius centered on Ursa Minor, and is within the sensitivity threshold of future detectors, such as GLAST .

sources.summary: The paper uses Ursa Minor as an observational constraint on tidal models, noting its old stellar population, estimated orbital eccentricity, high observed mass-to-light ratio, and low present stellar mass.

quotes.quote: The dSph satellite Ursa Minor contains only old stars with ages 15 Gyr (van den Bergh 1994). It's preliminary proper motion (without an uncertainty) is reported by Olszewski (1998), from which the orbital eccentricity is estimated. The dSph satellite Sculptor has stars with ages in the range 5-15 Gyr (van den Bergh 1994), and the proper motion has been estimated by Schweitzer et al. (1995). Both satellites are at a distance of 60-70 kpc. It is interesting to note that UMi has (M/L)_ obs =95 43 , while Scl has (M/L)_ obs =10.9 7.5 (Irwin Hatzidimitriou 1995).

The present masses of the stellar components (assuming (M/L)_ true =3 ) of UMi and Scl are, respectively, about 6 10^5 M_ and 4 10^6 M_ (Irwin Hatzidimitriou 1995), which may be too low to accrete gas from a hypothetical gas cloud on a similar orbit (.f. Magellanic or similar stream), or let alone retain gas over time-spans of many Gyr.

sources.summary: VERITAS observed Ursa Minor as a Local Group dwarf galaxy dark-matter target, noting existing spectroscopic data and reporting no significant gamma-ray emission with roughly 1 percent Crab flux upper limits.

quotes.quote: VERITAS undertook observations of three dwarf galaxies during the period 2007-2008: Draco, Ursa Minor, Willman I. Draco and Ursa Minor are relatively high surface brightness objects for which large spectroscopic data sets exist. Willman I belongs to the group of faint stellar systems that were recently discovered in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey willman05 . Although Willman I shares some similarities with tidally disrupted globular clusters, the extreme mass-to-light ratio inferred from its stellar velocity dispersion martin07 suggests that it is a dwarf galaxy. strigari08 have projected that this system may have significantly larger DM annihilation signal than that calculated for previously known dwarf galaxies.

VERITAS took 20 hours of observation on both Draco and Ursa Minor from March 2007 to May 2007 and 15 hours of observation on Willman I from December 2007 to Feburary 2008. No significant ray emission were detected, and the 95 C.L. upper limits on the integral ray flux from each source were set at the level of approximately 1 percent of the Crab Nebula flux.

sources.summary: The paper discusses Ursa Minor dwarf substructure, highlighting an off-centre stellar clump confirmed photometrically, indistinguishable metallicity, and a very cold kinematic feature.

quotes.quote: The presence of an off-centre localized stellar clump, reminiscent of those detected in dwarf elliptical galaxies (e.g. Binggeli et al. 2000), is probably clearest in the Ursa Minor dwarf (Olszewski Aaronson 1985; Irwin Hatzidmitriou 1995; Wyse et al. 2002; Palma et al. 2003), and has also been confirmed by deep photometric data taken with the Wield Field Planetary Camera (WFPC2) on the HST (Battinelli Demers 1999). This over-density locates 13'' ( 0.021 core radii) from the dwarf's centre. The metalicity of the stellar clump appears indistinguishible from the bulk population of the dwarf (Eskridge Schweitzer 2001), which only contains a single, old stellar population (e.g., Mateo 1998; Feltzing, Gilmore Wyse 1999). Interestingly, Kleyna et al. (2003) also reported the presence of a kinematic substructure in UMi that locates at a projected distance of 0.23 kpc from the dwarf's centre (i.e. 1.2 core radii) and has a very low velocity dispersion, 0.5 km/s.

sources.summary: The paper ranks Ursa Minor among the most promising known dwarf spheroidals for annihilation flux and notes VERITAS observations found no gamma-ray signal.

quotes.quote: The most promising source is Segue 1, followed by Ursa Major II, Ursa Minor, Draco, and Carina.
The median values of range from 5.0 km s ^ 1 (Leo IV) to 19 km s ^ 1 (Draco, Leo I), and of from 6.7 km s ^ 1 (Leo IV) to 30 km s ^ 1 (Ursa Minor, Carina).

The VERITAS array (consisting of four 144 m ^2 telescopes with a 3.5 ^ field of view and an energy threshold of 100 GeV ) has conducted a 15 hours observation of Willman 1 and 20 hour observations each of Draco and Ursa Minor . No ray signal was detected at a flux limit of 1 of the flux from the Crab Nebula, corresponding to a limit of 2.4 10^ 12 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 (95 confidence) at E>200 GeV .

sources.summary: The paper includes Ursa Minor as a dark-matter dominated bright dwarf spheroidal and says it is among the least luminous dwarfs yet consistent with one of the most massive inferred hosts.

quotes.quote: Our final sample therefore contains 9 dwarf spheroidals: Fornax, Leo I, Sculptor, Leo II, Sextans, Carina, Ursa Minor, Canes Venatici I, and Draco. All of these galaxies are known to be dark matter dominated at mateo1998 : wolf2010 find that their dynamical mass-to-light ratios at range from 10-300 .

Two of the three least luminous dwarfs, Draco and Ursa Minor, are consistent with the most massive hosts, while the three most luminous dwarfs (Fornax, Leo I, and Sculptor) are consistent with hosts of intermediate mass ( 18-20 ).
Ursa Minor and Draco, which are 40-80 times less luminous than Fornax, sit in halos that are comparable or slightly more massive, and therefore have inferred efficiencies of closer to =0.002 .

sources.summary: The paper uses Suzaku observations of Ursa Minor, a dark-matter-dominated dwarf spheroidal, to constrain moduli decay and compare its profile and surface density with Draco.

quotes.quote: We present the results of a search for such dark matter particles using spectra extracted from the first deep X-ray observations of the Draco and Ursa Minor dwarf spheroidal galaxies, which are dark matter dominated systems with extreme mass-to-light ratios and low intrinsic backgrounds.
The Ursa Minor and Draco systems, that have among the highest known dark matter surface densities , remain the only dwarf spheroidals observed by Suzaku to date.

The Draco and Ursa Minor profiles are very similar within 600 pc, consistent with an NFW profile with M_ 200 =3-30 10^9 M_ where M_ 200 is the mass encompassing an overdensity, relative to the critical density, of 200 , and a scale radius determined by the WMAP5 mass-concentration relation . Within apertures of 7.7' - the region from which most of the source flux originates -- and for distances of 77 (Ursa Minor) and 76 (Draco) kpc the corresponding dark matter surface density for a (tidal) truncation radius of 1.5 kpc is 150 50 M _ pc ^ 2 for each galaxy, where the cutoff is implemented using the `` n=2 BMO'' generalization of the NFW profile .

sources.summary: The paper includes Ursa Minor in its classical-dSph Jeans analysis and finds its soliton-plus-NFW transition radius is smaller than the stellar sample scales.

quotes.quote: More precisely, we solve axisymmetric Jeans equations and apply them to the kinematic data of the eight classical dSphs: Draco, Ursa Minor, Carina, Sextans, Leo I, Leo II, Sculptor and Fornax in order to investigate the global shape of ULA dark halo as well as to constrain on particle mass of ULA dark matter.

The Galactic Dwarf Spheroidal Galaxy Data In this work, we adopt the photometric and spectroscopic data of the resolved member stars in the eight luminous dSphs in the Milky Way: Draco, Ursa Minor, Carina, Sextans, Leo I, Leo II, Sculptor, and Fornax. This is because each have 200 stars with line-of-sight velocities from spectroscopy and well-constrained stellar distributions from photometry, so that these sample volumes might be enough to obtain more stringent limits on the mass range of m_ than those from other fainter dSphs such as ultra faint dwarf galaxies.
By contrast, however, it is found that for the case of Ursa Minor, Leo I and Fornax dSph, their transition radii are much smaller than their b_ and r_ 90 .

sources.summary: The paper describes the Ursa Minor dSph as a nearby classical dwarf spheroidal with identified blue-straggler candidates whose spatial distribution follows its old stellar population.

quotes.quote: The Ursa Minor dSph is a well-studied, relatively nearby, `classical' dwarf spheroidal (heliocentric distance of 75 kpc, Carrera2002 ). A possible blue straggler population in the Ursa Minor dSph was identified, by OA85 , in ground-based imaging that reached just below the dominant old main-sequence turn-off. Subsequent deep (small-area) imaging with the Hubble Space Telescope provided a cleaner CMD and again identified a blue straggler population RFGW2002 . The probable nature of this population as BSS, as opposed to genuinely younger stars, was established from wide-area ground-based imaging (out to the tidal limit) by Carrera2002 , using arguments based largely on their spatial distribution, which follows that of the old population see also Mapelli2007 .

sources.summary: The paper treats Ursa Minor (UMi) as an intact dwarf spheroidal comparator, noting I/II stars, Indus-overlapping process-enhanced metallicities, efficient-enrichment similarity, and an abundance knee at [Fe/H] = -2.2.

quotes.quote: Of the present-day intact dwarf spheroidal galaxies, a mixture of I and II stars has been discovered in three systems: Ursa Minor (UMi), Draco, and Fornax shetrone2001,cohen2009,letarte2010,lemasle2014 .

In metallicity, the Indus stream stars mostly overlap with the process enhanced stars found in Draco and UMi, whereas the I and II stars in the more massive Fornax galaxy generally have higher metallicities, and those in the small ultra-faint, Ret II galaxy are predominantly found at lower metallicities. This suggests that the Indus's progenitor was a relatively massive galaxy with efficient chemical enrichment, similar to UMi.

A recent abundance re-analysis of high-resolution data for stars in Fornax and UMi has found the knee in these two galaxies to be located at [ Fe/H ] = -1.6 and [ Fe/H ] = -2.2 , respectively, and predicted the location in Draco to be [ Fe/H ] = -2.1 reichert2020 .

sources.summary: The paper includes Ursa Minor in its BHB-based 3-D structure analysis and reports a distance gradient consistent with zero within 3 sigma.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage We combine the Dark Energy Camera Legacy Survey (DECaLS) DR8 photometry with Gaia photometry to study the 3-D structure of Bootes I, Draco, Ursa Minor, Sextans and Sculptor dwarf galaxies using blue horizontal branch (BHB) stars as distance indicators. We construct a new colour-absolute magnitude of BHB stars that we use to measure the distance gradients within the body of the dwarf galaxies.

In the case of dwarf galaxies Bootes I, Draco and Ursa Minor, the observed distance gradient is consistent with zero. Even though we do not observe statistically significant non-zero distance gradient in Bootes I, Draco and Ursa Minor, that is probably because the number of stars is too small and the uncertainty is too large to show any possible distance gradient.

sources.summary: The paper selects Ursa Minor as one of three nearby northern dwarf spheroidal targets for ASTRI Mini-Array dark-matter searches and simulated upper-limit constraints.

quotes.quote: In order to assess the capabilities of the ASTRI Mini-Array to search for DM in dSphs, in the context of the widely studied WIMP scenario (see Sect. indirectDM ), we consider three optimal targets observable from the Northern hemisphere: Ursa Minor (UMi), Coma Berenices (CBe), and Ursa Major II (UMa II). The targets were selected among the dSphs with the highest values of astrophysical factor, as reported in bon15b . The relevant kinematic (velocity dispersion _v , mass-to-light ratio M/L and ellipticity ) and brightness properties (half-light radius r_h and V band absolute magnitude M_V ) of the three selected targets are reported in Tab. tab:dsph .

We then simulate event lists for 100 h observations of UMi and CBe, and for both 100 h and 300 h in the case of UMa II; for this task, we assume the dSph halos to be point-like with respect to the ASTRI Mini-Array PSF of 0.1 deg.

sources.summary: The paper uses the UMi dwarf spheroidal galaxy as a low star-formation-rate system, comparing its low-mass stellar luminosity function with M92 and finding no detectable IMF difference.

quotes.quote: They compared the luminosity functions of unevolved, low mass stars in the UMi dSph galaxy and in the globular cluster M92. Both these systems are of similar age and metallicity, so the luminosity function comparison is an IMF comparison. The systems have star formation rates and present stellar density differing by about 6 orders of magnitude, and very different mass:light ratios. The results, shown in Fig.  2, show very clearly that there is no detectable IMF difference between these systems for stars with masses in the range 0.8 < M/M_ < 0.35 .

sources.summary: The paper searches Ursa Minor for diffuse ionized gas with WHAM, reports no detected emission, and derives upper limits on H-alpha intensity and ionized gas mass.

quotes.quote: The Wisconsin Mapper has been used to set the first deep upper limits on the intensity of diffuse emission from warm ionized gas in the Draco and Ursa Minor dwarf spheroidal galaxies. Assuming a velocity dispersion of 15 km s ^ 1 for the ionized gas, we set limits of I_ H 0.024 R and I_ H 0.021 R for the Draco and Ursa Minor dSphs, respectively, averaged over our 1 circular beam. Note that the Draco and Ursa Minor dSphs could contain substantial amounts of interstellar gas, equivalent to all of the gas injected by dying stars since the end of their main star forming episodes 8 Gyr in the past, without violating these limits on the mass of ionized gas.
We find upper limits of I_ H 0.024 R and I_ H 0.021 R for the Draco and Ursa Minor dSphs, respectively, averaged over the 1 circular WHAM beam.

sources.summary: The paper treats Ursa Minor as a dwarf spheroidal whose gas removal and early star-formation truncation could follow from early infall plus tidal and ram-pressure stripping.

quotes.quote: They find that if the progenitors of Draco or Ursa Minor fell into the Milky Way at z > 2 then ram pressure combined with tides was able to remove their entire gas content in a couple of orbits. This translates into a timescale of about 2-3 Gyr for a reasonable orbital time (namely one consistent with the current orbital distance). In this scenario the observed truncation of the star formation in Draco and Ursa Minor more than 10 Gyr ago occurred as a consequence of the infall of these galaxies into the Milky Way halo.
Dwarf spheroidals live in tidally truncated halos with moderate masses, 10^8-10^9 M_ , yet this implies M/L > 100 in some of them (e.g. Draco and Ursa Minor).

sources.summary: The paper uses Suzaku spectra of the Ursa Minor dwarf spheroidal galaxy to search for sterile-neutrino decay lines, finds no positive line detection, and derives new mixing-angle and mass constraints.

quotes.quote: We present results of our search for X-ray line emission associated with the radiative decay of the sterile neutrino, a well-motivated dark matter candidate, in Suzaku Observatory spectra of the Ursa Minor dwarf spheroidal galaxy. These data represent the first deep observation of one of these extreme mass-to-light systems and the first dedicated dark matter search using an X-ray telescope. No such emission line is positively detected, and we place new constraints on the combination of the sterile neutrino mass, m_ st , and the active-sterile neutrino oscillation mixing angle, .

sources.summary: The paper re-examines Ursa Minor as a classical dwarf spheroidal comparison target, using projected mass and distance to translate the Willman 1 line limits into an expected sterile-neutrino flux.

quotes.quote: Discussion Comparison with Limits from Classical Milky Way Dwarf Spheroidals We re-examine the Suzaku spectra of the Ursa Minor and Draco ``classical'' Milky Way dwarf spheroidals to check for consistency with the line flux limits of the 2.5 keV candidate sterile neutrino radiative decay emission line in Willman 1. The projected mass estimate adopted in LKB within the Suzkau spectral source region for Ursa Minor is 30 our fiducial value for the mass within the (smaller) Willman 1 Chandra extraction region. Draco has a similar projected mass to that of Ursa Minor str07 . Factoring in these mass ratios, and their respective distances, the 90 limits of the Willman 1 line flux translates to a predicted flux of (3.2 2.5) 10^ 5 \ photons \ cm ^ 2 \ s ^ 1 in Ursa Minor.

sources.summary: The paper treats Ursa Minor as an elongated, highly dark-matter-dominated dSph whose exceptional M/L is not fully explained by the simulations' tidal-stirring effects.

quotes.quote: When combined with ram pressure stripping and the effect of radiation fields at high redshift, such as the cosmic ionizing ultraviolet background, the tidal stirring mechanism can also account for both the low gas fraction and the extremely high DM content in some of the classic dSphs such as Draco and Ursa Minor Mayer_etal06,Mayer_etal07 .
This system could be the analogue of the most elongated dSphs in the LG such as Ursa Minor Irwin_Hatzidimitriou95 .
We stress at the outset that these increases of the M/L ratio are rather moderate and thus cannot account for the exceptional DM content in some of the classic dSphs such as Draco and Ursa Minor.

sources.summary: The paper models Ursa Minor as a classical dSph with possible cored dark-matter hints, ranking it a well-constrained, promising northern target with favorable J-factor uncertainty.

quotes.quote: There are two dSphs|Fornax and Ursa Minor|that show indirect hints of a cored distribution 2003ApJ...588L..21K,2006MNRAS.368.1073G ; however, in both cases the presence of a core is inferred based on a timing argument that assumes we are not catching the dSph at a special moment.

Our analysis places Ursa Minor as the best candidate for the northern sky (marginally better than Draco, which has long been a favourite target of northern hemisphere observatories) and Sculptor for the southern sky, when it comes to a favourable median and low uncertainty in the J factor.

With these criteria in mind, we find three categories: well-constrained and promising (Ursa Minor, Sculptor and Draco), well-constrained but less promising (Carina, Fornax and Leo I), and poorly constrained (Sextans and Leo II).

sources.summary: The paper finds Ursa Minor would need very large mass and lower proper motion to fit filamentary infall, and even then reaches smaller apocentres than Fornax or Sculptor.

quotes.quote: Here we test this scenario by making use of the proper motions of the Fornax, Sculptor, Ursa Minor and Carina dwarf spheroidals, and trace their orbits back through several variations of the Milky Way's potential and account for dynamical friction.
Regardless of the Galactic potential, Ursa Minor would have required a mass greater than 10^ 10 , even at 1 below the mean, to have fallen in along a filament from more than 200 kpc .
Ursa Minor and Carina would both require masses of the order of 10^ 10 and these masses come with the condition that the proper motions are at least 1 below the mean measurement.

sources.summary: The paper uses Ursa Minor as a low-stellar-mass dSph at about the same Milky Way distance as Draco, with early star-formation shutdown implying redshift-two accretion.

quotes.quote: For instance, Draco and Ursa Minor stopped forming stars 10 Gyr ago Grebel:1998uz , providing evidence for early accretion on to the MW.

As an example, we examine Draco and Ursa Minor, which have M_* 3 10^5 McConnachie:2012fh , are at similar distances D_ MW 76 McConnachie:2012fh and are expected to inhabit haloes with 25 . Both show SF occurring at early times and shutting down 10 Gyr ago Grebel:1998uz , implying their accretion around redshift two.

sources.summary: The paper notes no detected pulsars in Ursa Minor, classifies it with Draco as a high-J-factor classical dSph, and predicts MSP fluxes fainter than Fornax.

quotes.quote: No pulsars have been found yet in Ursa Minor, Draco, or Leo I with searches at 350 MHz using the Green Bank Radio Telescope Rubio-Herrera:2013 . Perhaps more importantly, Draco and Ursa Minor, two classical dSphs with large and well constrained J-factors e.g., Geringer-Sameth:2014yza , have estimated MSP fluxes another order of magnitude fainter than Fornax. The MSP emission of ultra-faint dSphs, such as Segue 1, is typically below 10^ 13 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 and likely beyond the reach of telescopes in the foreseeable future.

sources.summary: The paper models Ursa Minor as a dwarf spheroidal galaxy whose manganese and alpha-element abundances can be explained by rare Type Ia supernova subclasses.

quotes.quote: Therefore, we have included both in our stochastic chemical evolution model for the dwarf spheroidal galaxy Ursa Minor. The model predicts a butterfly-shape spread in [ Mn/Fe ] in the interstellar medium at low metallicity and - at the same time - a decrease of [ /Fe ] ratios at lower [ Fe/H ] than in the solar neighbourhood, both of which are consistent with the observed abundances in stars of Ursa Minor. The surprising agreement between our models and available observations provides a strong indication of the origins of these new sub-classes of SNe Ia.

sources.summary: The paper includes Ursa Minor among classical dwarf spheroidals with a shared alpha plateau, summarized elemental dispersions, and massive-dwarf Sr/Mg behavior.

quotes.quote: We show that once the NLTE effects are properly taken into account, all massive galaxies in our sample, that is, the MW halo and the classical dSphs Sculptor, Ursa Minor, Sextans, and Fornax, reveal a similar plateau at [ /Fe ] 0.3 for each of the process elements: Mg, Ca, and Ti.
Table Tab:mean summarizes the mean elemental ratios and their dispersions for the three galaxies with a sufficient number of stars: Sculptor, Ursa Minor, and the MW halo.

For Sr/Mg, there are two clear sequences at [ Mg/H ] 2.6 , one scattering around [ Mg/H ] 0.37 , which gathers the MW halo stars and the most massive (classical) dwarfs, such as Sextans, Ursa Minor and Sculptor, while the UFDs Leo IV, UMa II, and Bo otes I keep remarkably consistent and low abundance ratios, at the level of [ Sr/Mg ] 1.3 .

sources.summary: The paper describes Ursa Minor as a bright Milky Way dwarf spheroidal on a closer pericenter orbit, tied to higher dark-matter density and dense-subhalo TBTF constraints.

quotes.quote: The dSphs that have not come close to the Milky Way center (like Fornax, Carina and Sextans) are less dense in DM than those that have come closer (like Draco and Ursa Minor).

The inferred /m spanned a large range from 0.1 - 0.3 cm^2/g (Ursa Minor and Draco) to values greater than 10 cm^2/g (Fornax and Sextans), with a CDM concentration-mass prior from Vogelsberger:2015gpr .

To explain TBTF for the bright MW dSphs in light of the Gaia data using collisionless DM models, one must host Draco and Ursa Minor in the densest subhalos with pericenter distances close to 30 - 40 kpc, while explaining why Carina, Fornax and Sextans are hosted by lower-density subhalos that did not come close to the MW disk.

sources.summary: The paper includes Ursa Minor in a mass-dependent knee estimate, compares it with Sextans, and uses it to bound the stellar-mass range of abundance trends.

quotes.quote: By assuming a simple linear dependency of these quantities, we get a rough estimate of the knee (in Fornax, Sagittarius, Sextans, and Ursa Minor, see Table tab:alphaknee ): [ Fe/H ] _ ,knee,estimate (-0.10 0.03) M_V +(-3.00 0.25).

Following this idea, the knee at [ Fe/H ] _ Mg,knee =-2.0 may therefore be consistent with the similar luminous Ursa Minor that has not undergone a merger event (Fig. fig:alpha_knee_fit_mw_rel ), whereas the more metal-poor knee may be formed before the accretion.
The trends did not show any mass dependence within the statistics of our study in a stellar mass range of 2.1 10^7 M _ 2.9 10^5 M _ spanned by Sagittarius and Ursa Minor, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper places Ursa Minor among the classical dwarf spheroidals that prefer cusped dark-matter halos, with results robust to the tested prior change.

quotes.quote: Secondly, Ursa Minor, Leo I and Leo II also prefer cusped dark matter halos, although the uncertainties for the inner slope, , are larger than for Draco.

It is found from this figure that the galaxies having a cusped dark matter halo like Draco and Ursa Minor are not so much affected by new prior, whilst the effect of new prior makes Fornax and Sextans less dense core. Therefore, we bear in mind that Fornax and Sextans are possible to have a cored dark matter density. On the other hand, we can confirm that our results for Draco and Ursa Minor have cusped dark matter halos.

sources.summary: The paper discusses Ursa Minor as a low-mass dwarf spheroidal with a possible abundance knee and a chemical locus outside the GE/S-like region.

quotes.quote: Indeed, previous studies of stars in Draco, Sextans and Ursa Minor shetrone2003vlt , Sculptor Hill2019 , and Sextans theler2020chemical suggest the presence of a ``knee'' at [ Fe/H ] -2.

Based on a compilation of chemical compositions from various works CohenHuang2009,CohenHuang2010,Starkenburg2013 , quote [ Fe/H ] _ knee 1.9 for Sculptor, Ursa Minor, and Fornax and [ Fe/H ] _ knee 2.5 for Draco and Carina see also deboer2014 .

Differences between the loci occupied by GE/S and lower mass satellites are even more pronounced, particularly for Draco, Carina, Sextans and Ursa Minor, whose sample stars are predominantly located outside the ``chemically unevolved'' region.

sources.summary: The review notes early process-enhanced stars and thorium measurements in Ursa Minor, and says its europium trends indicate stochastic process enrichment.

quotes.quote: At first, very few stars were discovered with clear process-enhanced nucleosynthetic signature outside the stellar halo (in the dwarf galaxy Ursa Minor and the globular cluster M15; Otsuki06, aoki2007_cos82, Cohen2009 ).

So far, thorium has been measured in a number of stars in several dwarf galaxies: Reticulum II Ji2018 , Ursa Minor aoki2007_cos82 , Fornax Reichert2021 , Sagittarius Hansen2020 , and the disrupted dwarf galaxy Indus Hansen2021 .
Ursa Minor is dominated by process enrichment, but it has unusual Eu trends and large scatter that likely suggest it is still dominated by stochastic process enrichment Cohen2010,McWilliam2018 .

sources.summary: The paper treats Ursa Minor as a classical dwarf plausibly linked to the Magellanic system, tied to the stream plane and a roughly even association probability.

quotes.quote: In particular, four of the classical dwarfs -- Carina, Draco, Fornax and Ursa Minor -- each have a 50 probability of once belonging to the Magellanic system, thus providing a possible explanation for the ``plane of satellites'' conundrum.

Previous work LyndenBell1976 noticed that the dwarf galaxies Draco, Ursa Minor and Sculptor lie close to the plane of the Magellanic gas stream, itself discovered shortly before Mathewson1974 , and proposed that they are tidally stripped satellites of the postulated Great Magellanic Galaxy -- the precursor of the LMC itself.

In particular, four of the classical dSph -- Carina, Draco, Fornax and Ursa Minor -- have a roughly equal chance to either be accreted with the Magellanic system or originate from elsewhere (in case of Carina, the chances are even higher, 2:1 ).

sources.summary: The paper finds Ursa Minor among the strongest dwarf-spheroidal targets, giving the best single-source limits and weaker combined constraints only when stacked.

quotes.quote: Different sources have different profile parameters ( _s , r_s ), diffusion radii ( r_h ), and distances to the Earth, which affect the final constraints on the DM parameters. The SEDs of the 8 sources show differences because of these factors ( fig:8radiation ). In fig:8radiation we show the SEDs of the 8 different sources from the annihilation through the b b channel with a cross section of v = 10^ 26 cm ^ 3 s ^ 1 . We can see that Sculptor and Ursa Minor have stronger fluxes than others, making them the best targets to constrain DM with this method.

For single-source analysis, we find that the observation of Ursa Minor gives the strongest constraints (as shown in fig:8radiation , the expected signal from Ursa Minor is one of the highest among the 8 sources for a given cross-section). The combined analysis of eight dSphs yields constraints about three times stronger than those of Ursa Minor.

sources.summary: The paper uses Ursa Minor as a benchmark high-M/L dwarf spheroidal, arguing that similarly dense satellites can survive near the Milky Way and still require substantial dark matter.

quotes.quote: We show that only dwarfs with central dark matter densities as high as those of Draco and Ursa Minor can survive for 10 Gyr in the proximity of the Milky Way: this is naturally achieved within hierarchical models, where the densest objects should have small orbital times due to their early formation epochs.

Moreover, both dSphs and dIrrs are dark matter dominated and show an anti-correlation between their mass-to-light ratio and their luminosity (Mateo 1998): Draco and Ursa Minor, the faintest dSphs ( M_B > -10 ), have M/L > 25 (Lake 1990)) and the dimmest dIrrs, GR8 and SagDIG ( M_B > -12 ) have M/L 30 .

Although not negligible, such tidal effects cannot rule out the need of high dark matter contents in some of the dSphs: objects like Draco and Ursa Minor ( M/L > 25 ) would still have M/L > 12 even with the maximum velocity dispersion enhancement just described.

sources.summary: The paper cites Ursa Minor's cold stellar substructure as evidence for a cored dark halo, noting simulations where the structure quickly dissolves in a cusp but survives in a uniform-density core.

quotes.quote: The recent identification of a kinetically cold stellar substructure in the Ursa Minor dSph (Kleyna et al. 2003) strongly suggests that the dark halo of that galaxy has a central core. Using N-body simulations Kleyna et al. (2003) showed that this cold structure would survive for less than 1 Gyr if the dark halo is cusped. Only if the dark halo has a uniform density core can the cold substructure survive.

sources.summary: Ursa Minor is grouped with nearby high-M/L dwarf spheroidals where extra-tidal stars would argue against dominant dark matter and significant dark matter content.

quotes.quote: Carina, Ursa Minor and Draco, on the other hand, cannot contain a dominating dark matter component if the observed 'extra-tidal' stars are located exterior to the tidal radii of these systems.

Even with the large error bars, which are due to the large observational uncertainties in the orbital and structural parameters, figure 1 clearly demonstrates that in this case Carina, Draco and Ursa Minor could not be dark matter dominated (see also Moore 1996).
The existence of 'extra-tidal' stars in Carina, Draco and Ursa Minor therefore would demonstrate that these dSphs cannot contain significant amounts of dark matter.

gmw_00000579.name: NGC 4874
gmw_00000579.overview: NGC 4874 is a central cD brightest cluster galaxy in the Coma cluster [arXiv:1310.3755, arXiv:1612.08089]. It is a radio galaxy hosting a wide-angle-tail radio source [arXiv:1002.0594, arXiv:2207.06624] and a compact central X-ray gas corona [arXiv:2207.06624].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4874 as a central Coma cD galaxy with disproportionate stellar mass, intracluster-light evidence, central-core structure, and a large fitted effective radius.

quotes.quote: Objects assigned to class G2 (photometric ellipticals) have single S ersic index n > n_ disk max and include two known central cD galaxies, NGC 4874 and NGC 4889. We label these two sources separately as cD galaxies because they contain a disproportionately large fraction of the stellar mass.

Of the three cD galaxies in Coma, two (NGC 4874 and NGC 4889) lie in the projected central 0.5 Mpc and are therefore in our sample. The third cD (NGC 4839) lies is in the outer southwest region of Coma and is not part of this study. Definitive proof that NGC 4874 and NGC 4889 are cDs is the detection of intra-cluster light in Coma (Kormendy Bahcall 1974; Melnick et al. 1977; Thuan Kormendy 1977; Bernstein et al. 1995; Adami et al. 2005; Arnaboldi 2011). The single S ersic indices reported in Appendix decomp and Table 1cptab for the these cD galaxies are n 3-4.4 because the decompositions also include the central core. The central core is a clear deviation from the inward extrapolation of the S ersic profile that characterizes the outer galaxy structure.
For NGC 4874, we measure the r_ e and n of NGC 4874 to be 17.3 kpc and 3.05, respectively, while Hoyos et al. (2011) measure r_ e and n to be 3.2 kpc and 1.3.

sources.summary: Chandra data show NGC 4874 is a Coma brightest cluster galaxy with an X-ray corona, no detected cavities, a wide-angle-tail radio source, and a possible hotter coronal component.

quotes.quote: We use deep Chandra X-ray Observatory observations to examine the coronae of the two brightest cluster galaxies in the Coma cluster of galaxies, NGC 4874 and NGC 4889.

In contrast, NGC 4874 does not show any evidence for cavities, although it hosts a well-known wide-angle-tail radio source which is visible outside the region occupied by the X-ray corona. These two galaxies show that AGN feedback can behave in varied ways in the same cluster environment. galaxies: ISM --- X-rays: galaxies --- X-rays: galaxies: clusters Contrary to theoretical expectations, it was found that the two dominant central galaxies in the Coma cluster, NGC 4874 and NGC 4889, showed extended X-ray emission from their interstellar medium Vikhlinin01 .

The spectrum of NGC 4874 allows the inclusion of a second thermal component with a temperature of around 2 keV. The addition of this component significantly reduces the ^2 of the fit and raises the best fitting metallicity to 1.3 (assuming both coronal components have the same metallicity). The hotter second component is likely due to material at larger radius in the corona.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4874 as a diffuse cD galaxy whose halo, large break radius, and steeper cusp imply SMBH scouring plus likely later hot-gas accretion.

quotes.quote: NGC 4874 on the other hand has a much more diffuse appearance and is thus classified as a cD-type galaxy. If we apply our results to this galaxy, we find a different history. Its central black hole was not formed during a massive quasar phase but instead by the merger of several very massive progenitor galaxies. This gave rise to its extended halo as well as a very large break radius of R_ b = 1730 pc 2007ApJ...664..226L formed by SMBH scouring. Its central cusp profile, =0.12 , is steeper than that of NGC 4889.

Assuming its initial profile was flatter in the past (e.g. comparable to that of NGC 4889), hot gas accretion was likely relevant in this galaxy. We can estimate the central SMBH mass in NGC 4874 from its break radius, the empirical R_ b relation given in and add the mass required to increase its central slope by =0-0.09 (taken from our Model 7).

sources.summary: NGC 4874 is treated as the central Coma cD galaxy with satellite companions, a cD envelope, and a globular cluster distribution transitioning to intragalactic clusters beyond 300 kpc.

quotes.quote: The giants NGC 4874 and NGC 4889 are the dominant galaxies in the rich and well known Coma cluster (A1656), for which we adopt d = 100 Mpc, (m-M)_I = 35.02 , and E_ B-V = 0.01 . Reproductions of the NGC 4889 and NGC4874-F2 fields from the ACS/WFC camera are shown in Figures fig:ngc4889 and fig:ngc4874 . NGC 4874 is surrounded by a handful of smaller satellite galaxies see Fig. 1 of for their identification numbers cho_etal2016 , though as will be seen below these do not contribute noticeably to the overall GC population, with the exception of a small excess around NGC 4873 (at left center in Fig. fig:ngc4874 ). Though NGC 4874 and 4889 have similar V- band luminosities, NGC 4874 is clearly the one lying at or near the center of the Coma potential well (as defined by the intracluster X-ray gas) and has a cD-type envelope.

Their analysis shows that NGC 4874 is essentially at the center of the GC distribution in Coma and that its own GC spatial profile makes a clear transition to a newly discovered Intra-Galactic cluster (IGC) population, which becomes dominant beyond a projected radius of 300 kpc. In the CDF, two modes (MP and MR) are clearly present and the MP mode is much more dominant for the IGC. Color-magnitude diagrams for the GC populations around both NGC 4874 and 4889 from HST ACS imaging are presented and discussed briefly by lee_jang2016a , while cho_etal2016 complete a more comprehensive discussion specifically for the NGC 4874 system now including 3-color (g,I,H) photometry.

sources.summary: Chandra resolves 4874 as one of Coma's dominant galaxies retaining a compact warm X-ray gas corona, with faint extended emission and measured soft X-ray flux.

quotes.quote: The total exposure was 48 ksec for 4874 and 56 ksec for 4889. The central part of the composite ACIS-I image is shown in Fig. fig:big . Notice the faint arcmin-scale X-ray enhancements around 4874 and 4889, previously detected by . At the center of these extended structures there are bright (500 counts) compact sources coincident with the optical positions of the galaxies.

The observed 0.5--2 keV flux is 3.9 0.1 10^ 14 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 for 4874 \ and 3.2 0.1 10^ 14 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 for 4889, corresponding to luminosities in the same band of 9.1 10^ 40 and 7.6 10^ 40 erg s ^ 1 . The enlarged X-ray and optical images of 4874 and 4889 are shown in Fig. fig:panels . The X-ray sources associated with the galaxies are extended, but unlike most ellipticals, the X-ray emission is much more compact than the distribution of optical light.

sources.summary: The paper uses deep archival HST imaging of NGC 4874, a central Coma cD galaxy, to compare its globular-cluster luminosity functions with those of NGC 4921.

quotes.quote: For comparison, we studied two more Coma galaxies for which deep images are available in the archive: NGC 4874 and NGC 4889, two cD galaxies in the central region of Coma. We analyzed the HST/ACS F475W (g) and F814W (I) images of NGC 4874 and NGC 4889, in the archive (PIDs : 11711 and 10861). We combined the images of these galaxies with a pixfrac value of 1.0, and a pixel scale of 0 05 for F475W and 0 03 for F814W.

The total exposure times of NGC 4874 are 5,071 s for F475W and 11,825 s for F814W, and those of NGC 4889 are 4,770 s for F475W and 9,960 s for F814W. These exposure times are much shorter than those for the NGC 4921 images. However, the images of these elliptical galaxies suffer much less crowding than those of NGC 4921, which is an almost face-on spiral galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 4874's rich globular-cluster system, finding optical color bimodality, a strong blue tilt, extended blue clusters, and an offset GC-system center toward NGC 4872.

quotes.quote: We also find a very strong color-magnitude trend, or ``blue tilt,'' for the blue component of the optical color distribution of the NGC 4874 GC system. A similarly strong trend is present for the overall mean \ color as a function of magnitude; for <-10 mag, these trends imply a steep mass-metallicity scaling with Z M_ GC ^ 1.4 0.4 , but the scaling is not a simple power law and becomes much weaker at lower masses. As in other similar systems, the spatial distribution of the blue GCs is more extended than that of the red GCs, partly because of blue GCs associated with surrounding cluster galaxies. In addition, the center of the GC system is displaced by 4 1 kpc towards the southwest from the luminosity center of NGC 4874, in the direction of NGC 4872.

We have studied the rich GC system of NGC 4874, the cD galaxy in the core of the Coma cluster of galaxies, using optical \ \ and \ imaging from the ACS/WFC and near-IR \ imaging from WFC3/IR. The GC system of NGC 4874 and the surrounding Coma core was previously studied in \ and \ as part of the ACSCCS (Peng \ 2011), and we find excellent photometric agreement with that study, but the exposure time of our \ observations is more than seven times that of the ACSCCS imaging, giving a limiting magnitude more than a magnitude fainter in this bandpass.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4874 as a central D galaxy in Coma whose associated group and surrounding diffuse light trace the cluster's merger-driven intracluster-light morphology.

quotes.quote: A formation scenario that is currently contemplated for the Coma cluster is a merger of two sub-clusters or groups associated with the two D galaxies, NGC 4874 and NGC 4889, possibly after their second core crossing briel1992,watt1992,davis1993,colless1996,arnaud2001,neumann2003,adami2005b,gerhard2007 . Most dynamical and X-ray studies suggest that the group related to NGC 4874 could have been at the center of the main cluster potential in the past, being displaced out of the bottom of the potential well by the infall of the group associated with NGC 4889 e.g. davis1993,colless1996,gerhard2007 .

We observe that the diffuse light is mainly spread over a common envelope containing the two giant galaxies NGC 4874 (right below the masked, very bright star with spikes) and NGC 4889 (east of NGC 4874). The ICL distribution is clearly elongated toward east, as reported by many authors in the literature kormendy1974,mattila1977,thuan1977,adami2005 . This lack of symmetry confirms again that Coma is far from being relaxed. For wavelengths redder than the J0430 band, we see a clear arc-shaped extension of diffuse light spreading to the southwest from NGC4874 as in welch1971,kormendy1974,mattila1977,melnick1977 , which is associated with a change in the ICL position angle, from 80 degrees in the narrow blue filters to 55-65 degrees in redder bands.

sources.summary: The paper discusses NGC 4874 as one of Coma's dominant central galaxies, using its optical velocity and associated substructure to interpret the cluster gas velocity and merger history.

quotes.quote: The Coma cluster , Abell 1656 , contains two dominant galaxies, which are NGC 4874 and NGC 4889 . The two galaxies have a large velocity difference of 700 km s ^ 1 between them FitchettWebster87 , likely caused by a major merger in the cluster.

The results show that we obtain gas velocities which are very similar to the optical velocities of the two central galaxies, appearing to cluster around these values. The regions in the centre, to the west, to the south-west and to the south have velocities consistent with NGC 4889 , while those to the north-west, north, north-east, east and south-east appear to better match NGC 4874 .

In the Coma cluster, we find that the velocity of the gas is close to the optical velocities of the two central galaxies. The ICM to the north and east of the core has a velocity close to that of NGC 4874 , while that to the south and south-west has a lower velocity similar to NGC 4889 .

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4874 as a Coma BCG requiring HST-based dynamical modelling; its dark matter fraction is consistent between models and its black hole is very massive.

quotes.quote: For the two BCGs, NGC 4874 and NGC 4889, additional steps were taken to ensure high-quality dynamical models can be created. For these dynamical models, we replace the MGE results taken from the SDSS photometric data with that from the higher-resolution HST data. These new models permit us to generate higher resolution dynamical models of the galaxies in the region covered by the IFU.

Next, we focus on the two BCGs in the cluster, NGC 4889 and NGC 4874, which are the two most massive galaxies in Fig. dm_frac . We observe that the dark matter fraction for NGC 4874 (the less massive of the two BCGs) is consistent for both sets of dynamical models, but for NGC 4889 we see a drastic difference between the models.

The massive BH of 1.4 10^ 10 M_ for NGC4874 appears to confirm the finding that massive BCGs have larger BHs than expected for their velocity dispersion, due to their dry-mergers accretion history (e.g. sec. 6.7.2 of KormendyHo2013 ).

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 4874 is a Coma brightest cluster galaxy whose core IMF is consistent with a Kroupa-like Milky Way form, unlike NGC 4889.

quotes.quote: We include the two Coma BCGs in our sample, NGC 4874 and NGC 4889, previously studied by Z17. For NGC 4874 we confirm the result of Z17, that the IMF in the core is consistent with a Kroupa parameterization. However, for NGC 4889 we find evidence for a bottom-heavy IMF, in contrast to Z17.

As the spectral extraction is weighted toward pixels with the highest S/N, our effective aperture within the longslit is 0.7'' 4.12'' for NGC 4889 and 0.7'' 3.51'' for NGC 4874, corresponding to <0.07R_ e for NGC 4874 and <0.11R_ e for NGC 4889 (Table tab:sample ). This is somewhat smaller than the aperture of Z17 ( 0.2R_ e , see their Table 1), but both are within the region where bottom-heavy IMF signatures are typically observed van_Dokkum_2017 . Moreover, our data for NGC 4874 are more centrally concentrated than for NGC 4889, so this is unlikely to explain the discrepancy with Z17 for the latter object.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC4874 as the main central galaxy of Coma, linking nearby X-ray excess and central temperature and abundance structure to its galaxy halo and relaxed cluster core.

quotes.quote: As already proposed by Colless Dunn ( ), our data suggest that NGC4874 is simply the central galaxy of the main Coma cluster, rather than being associated with a second subgroup in an early merging stage with the main cluster (as proposed by Donnelly ). Several facts support this picture. First the remarkably homogeneous temperature distribution within the central <10' region suggests the gas in that region is basically in a relaxed state.

The excess (size 3' in radius) around NGC4874, when subtracting a model (representative of the large scale morphology) is a natural consequence for this type of morphology. Moreover, part of the excess is certainly due to the contribution of the halo of the galaxy itself. The very significant drop in temperature within 1'= 40 kpc of NGC4874 (Fig. fig:fkTprof ) is natural in that context, as well as the increase in abundance, which could be due to an enriched ISM.

sources.summary: The paper measures K-band surface brightness fluctuations for NGC 4874, the dominant Coma elliptical, deriving its distance and noting its X-ray-centered, radio-source role in the cluster potential.

quotes.quote: We have used the Keck I Telescope to measure K band surface brightness fluctuations (SBFs) of NGC 4874, the dominant elliptical galaxy in the Coma cluster. We use deep \ WFPC2 optical imaging to account for the contamination due to faint globular clusters and improved analysis techniques to derive measurements of the SBF apparent magnitude. Using a new SBF calibration which accounts for the dependence of K band SBFs on the integrated color of the stellar population, we measure a distance modulus of 34.99 0.21 mag ( 100 10 Mpc) for the Coma cluster.

The core of the Coma cluster is dominated in luminosity by two supergiants, the elliptical NGC 4889 ( B_T=12.53 mag) and the cD NGC 4874 ( B_T=12.63 mag; RC3 ). Like many Abell clusters, Coma possesses significant substructure e.g., 1993AJ....105..409D, 1993ApJ...413..492M . However, NGC 4874 appears to be special for two reasons: it is located at peak of the diffuse X-ray emission 1993MNRAS.261L...8W , and it has a strong nuclear radio source characteristic of many central giant ellipticals 1987ApJ...315L..29H .

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4874 as a central Coma cD galaxy whose subcluster core may remain self-bound while interacting with the NGC 4889 subcluster.

quotes.quote: A substructure is present at 5000 , probably from in-fall of a galaxy group, while the main intracluster stellar component is centered around 6500 , 700 offset from the nearby cD galaxy NGC 4874. The kinematics and morphology of the intracluster stars show that the cluster core is in a highly dynamically evolving state. In combination with galaxy redshift and X-ray data this argues strongly that the cluster is currently in the midst of a subcluster merger, where the NGC 4874 subcluster core may still be self-bound, while the NGC 4889 subcluster core has probably dissolved.

The other supergiant galaxy in the core, NGC 4874, is often regarded as the true nucleus of the Coma cluster in these scenarios. This is based on the fact that NGC 4874 is surrounded by the largest concentration in the projected distribution of galaxies Biviano96 , as well as to some extent on the fact that it has a relatively large diffuse light halo. The fact that its radial velocity is significantly offset relative to the velocities of nearby galaxies is then attributed to the interaction with NGC 4889, which could have perturbed NGC 4874 out of the cluster core Colless96 .

sources.summary: The paper links NGC 4874 to an infalling Coma group whose stripped cool gas, bow shock, velocity offset, and relatively unperturbed state suggest pre-core-passage minor merger activity.

quotes.quote: The simplest interpretation of our temperature maps, in agreement with B96, is that Coma is experiencing a merger with a moderately sized group associated with NGC 4874 located south of the observed hot spot. The hot spot of emission is due to a bow shock generated by the group as it moves through Coma's intercluster medium(ICM). As the group has fallen toward the core of Coma, it has left a trail of cool gas delineating its in-bound track, which we see as the filament detected by V97.

Alternatively, the presence of a cD galaxy in the form of NGC 4874 could indicate that the group is in an early stage of the merger prior to entering the core, and as such has not yet been disrupted by the merger process. B96 separated the cluster galaxies into ``bright'' and ``faint'' samples, and found-- within the ``bright'' sample-- two galaxy groups, one associated with each of the two dominant galaxies. This is similar to the results of Fitchett Webster (1987).

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC4874 as the second-brightest central Coma BCG, with an extended envelope, no detected rotation or substructure, and H measurements matching literature data.

quotes.quote: NGC4874 This galaxy has a large, extended envelope, and is the second brightest galaxy of the famous pair of BCGs at the centre of the Coma cluster. The surface brightness profile was published by Peletier et al. \ (1990). No significant rotation or velocity substructure is detected for this galaxy (Paper 1). Trager et al. \ (2008) compiled a list of all the central H measurements for this galaxy, and found an error weighted mean value of 1.57 0.05 (including data from Fisher et al. \ 1995; Trager et al. \ 1998; J rgensen 1999; Kuntschner et al. \ 2001; Moore et al. \ 2002; Nelan et al. \ 2005; S a nchez-Bl a zquez et al. \ 2006b). The H index measured here (1.630 0.082 ) compares very well with the literature data.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 5C4.85 with NGC 4874, the Coma central cD galaxy, and describes its wide-angle-tail radio morphology, lobe size, and polarization.

quotes.quote: 5C4.85 - NGC 4874 This a cluster source, optically identified with the Coma central cD galaxy NGC 4874 (see e.g. Mehlert et al. 2000). It is a Wide Angle Tail radio galaxy, whose maximum angular extension is 30 '' , corresponding to 15 kpc. The angular extension of the two lobes individually is larger at the lowest frequency. The northern lobe shows a mean fractional polarization of 10 and 11 at 4.535 and 8.465 GHz respectively, while the western lobe is less polarized ( 7 at both frequencies). In Fig. fig:85_pol the radio emission is shown at 4.535 and 8.465 GHz.

sources.summary: The paper discusses cD galaxy NGC 4874 in Coma as offset from the main intracluster stellar component and merging with the NGC 4889 subcluster.

quotes.quote: (1995) and 5 arcmin away from the cD galaxy NGC 4874. A sub-structure is present at 5000 km s^ 1 , probably from in-fall of a galaxy group, while the main intracluster stellar component is centered around 6500 km s^ 1 , 700 km s^ 1 offset from the nearby cD galaxy NGC 4874 ( v_ sys = 7224 km s ^ 1 ; from NED) .

The NGC 4889 sub-cluster is likely to have fallen into Coma from the eastern A2199 filament, in a direction nearly in the plane of the sky, meeting the NGC 4874 sub-cluster arriving from the west. The two inner sub-cluster cores are presently beyond their first and second close passage, during which the elongated distribution of diffuse light has been created.

In the Coma cluster, the elongated morphology and kinematics of the ICL, the galaxy morphology and X-ray data are consistent with the merging of NGC 4889 and NGC 4874 along a binary orbit, the two galaxies being currently observed beyond their first and second close passage. the Hydra and Fornax clusters show evidence for un-mixed stellar components coming from accretion/tidal stripping events in their cores.

sources.summary: The paper classifies GMP3329 as an undisputed slow rotator in Coma with no detected rotation, but notes its dispersion map is moderate compared with GMP2921.

quotes.quote: If we select SRs morphologically based on the absence of significant rotation we find four examples: GMP2921, GMP3329, GMP3792 and GMP2975. If we classify SRs as all galaxies with < , then there are also four examples in Fig. fig:lam-eps : GMP2921, GMP3329, GMP3792 and GMP3433.

GMP2921 and GMP3329 show no evidence of rotation and are undisputed SRs. Similarly GMP3792 and GMP2975 show no evidence of global rotation extending to large radii. All four of these morphological SRs have <0.1 .

The majority of the dispersion maps in Fig. fig:kina are blue (dynamically cold, <100 ), while one dispersion map stands out from all the others as being dynamically much hotter than the others; that of GMP2921. This galaxy is likely the most massive in the cluster and is one of the two central dominant galaxies. However, the other centrally dominant galaxy, GMP3329, has a very moderate dispersion map in comparison; indeed the dispersion map of the non-central galaxy GMP3792 is just as dynamically hot.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4874 as a Coma BCG/cD galaxy anchoring a separate subcluster; ICL2 and ICL3 trace its halo in the ongoing Coma core merger.

quotes.quote: We present optical spectra of three ICL regions in the Coma cluster, located between 100-180 kpc from their nearest brightest cluster galaxies (BCGs): NGC 4889 and NGC 4874.

Furthermore, the core of the cluster comprises two giant cD galaxies, NGC 4889 and NGC 4874, and X–ray observations Adami2005 suggest that these galaxies are likely associated with two distinct substructures. The line-of-sight velocity distributions of intracluster planetary nebulae in the central region of Coma Gerhard2007 also suggest an ongoing merger of distinct substructures associated with NGC 4889 and NGC 4874.

This shows that stars in ICL1 are tracing a massive dark matter halo around NGC 4889, while stars in ICL2 and ICL3 are tracing a distinct halo around NGC 4874. Our results confirm that NGC 4889 and NGC 4874 not only originate in separate clusters, but that those clusters are still distinct at the present day.

sources.summary: The paper links NGC 4874 to the most extended Coma globular-cluster overdensity, a common envelope with nearby NGC galaxies, and a cluster structure over five times the galaxy-light radius.

quotes.quote: NGC \ 4874, NGC \ 4871, NGC \ 4872, and NGC \ 4873 appear to share a common envelope in one of the main agglomerations of globular clusters. There is a clear bridge of globular clusters between NGC \ 4889 and NGC \ 4874.

As shown in Figure 5 and Table 2, globular clusters associated with NGC 4874 have an extended spatial structure with an effective radius that is more than five times the effective radius of the galaxy light. The NGC 4874 overdensity of globular clusters is the largest in the core of Coma -- larger than the overdensity associated with NGC 4889 or IC 4051.
The most extended overdensity of globular clusters is associated with NGC \ 4874.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 4874 as a large-core Coma elliptical with intermediate-scale and halo components, outwardly rising velocity dispersion, and bluer outer colors.

quotes.quote: Akin to NGC 4486, the giant elliptical NGC 4874 is the second brightest galaxy sitting at the center of the Coma cluster. HST WFPC2 F606W images were used for 2/12 galaxies with no obvious dust absorption (NGC 4874 and NGC 4889). We find that the light profiles for two sample galaxies (NGC 4874 and A3571-BCG) are well described by a core-S ersic spheroid, a S ersic intermediate-scale component and an outer exponential halo model.

In Section  Sec5.3 , we show that the colors for these two objects gradually turn bluer towards larger radii, consistent with our multi-component light profile decompositions. In addition, 2017MNRAS.464..356V showed that the velocity dispersion of NGC 4874 increases outward from 240 km s ^ 1 near the center to 350 km s ^ 1 at R 60 , akin to that of the large-core galaxy IC 1101 having an intermediate-scale component.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4874 as a Coma BCG with weak but clear prolate rotation, a lopsided northeast stellar halo, and faint shells visible in residuals.

quotes.quote: NGC 4874 has weak but clear prolate rotation. It is one of two dominant members of the Coma cluster (Abell 1656), and it is considered a BCG e.g., liu10 . The stellar halo of the galaxy is highly asymmetrical, lopsided towards the northeast direction.

Fortunately, the lopsided halo becomes evident in the SDSS image, the left panel of Fig. fig:4874 . The galaxy also possesses faint shells that are barely visible in the Legacy Surveys image, but they are present when looking at the residuals available in the Legacy Surveys viewer, the right panel of Fig. fig:4874 .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4874 as a dominant Coma BCG and radio galaxy with WAT jets, a compact X-ray corona, and energy deposition outside the corona.

quotes.quote: 16: NGC 4874 One of the two dominant BCGs of the Coma cluster BaierTiersch1990 is a low-ionization nuclear emission-line region galaxy KentandGunn . The optical host is a regular low brightness elliptical galaxy 2000A&A...362..871C . Unlike many relaxed clusters that host a single BCG, Coma has two very bright galaxies in the core - NGC 4889 and NGC 4874. While the former is brighter by 0.5 magnitude, the latter is rounder and possesses a rich system of satellite galaxies. NGC 4874 is also a prominent radio galaxy, which is yet another characteristic feature of giant ellipticals residing at the bottom of cluster potential wells Burns1990 . The source is located close to the peak of the distributions of galaxies and X-ray gas. The radio source has a wide-angle tail (WAT) radio morphology and its projected maximum angular extension is 30 arcsec, corresponding to 15 kpc. We detect the radio core and two radio jets forming a wide angle between them in both of our 250--500 MHz and 550--850 MHz band images. The largest projected linear extent of the source in the 250--500 MHz band is 0 81 ( 22.7 kpc). This one of two core BCGs of the Coma cluster, NGC 4874 also retains its central X-ray gas corona, i.e., the interstellar gas with a temperature of 1--2 keV is confined by the hot intergalactic medium of the Coma Cluster into a compact ( 3 kpc) cloud. The radio jets are found to be anti-correlated with the X-ray emission, and there are also small X-ray indentations of the nucleus, where the jets leave the coronal gas (see also Section sec:discuss ). It seems that the supermassive black hole in NGC 4874 is depositing its energy outside of the corona, and it may be ineffective in preventing the cooling taking place Vikhlininetal,Sandersetal .

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4874/GMP 3329 as the Coma cD galaxy, requiring special sky subtraction and a larger aperture because of its projected size.

quotes.quote: The design that preserved the preferred east-west orientation of the slit (to minimise atmospheric refraction effects) and also maximised the number of ETGs along the slit length covered a region around the cD galaxy GMP 3329 (=NGC 4874).

However, for NGC 4874, which did fill its slitlet, and for D128 and NGC 4872, whose spectra were contaminated by that of NGC 4874, sky subtraction was performed first using the `sky' information at the edge of their slitlets and then corrected by comparing this sky spectrum to the average sky from all other slitlets.

Various apertures were used to extract the spectra: apertures with equivalent circular diameters of 2 0 , 2 7 , 3 2 , 3 4 , 3 6 , 3 8 , 4 5 , and a `physical' aperture of 2 \ diameter for all galaxies except GMP 3329 (=NGC 4874). For this galaxy, an aperture of 8 \ diameter was used due its large projected size (note that an aperture of 8 \ is too small to be extracted reliably for many of the galaxies in our sample: for example, 8 =0 33 for GMP 3534; Table tbl:sample ).

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC4874 as the second-brightest cD in Coma’s famous central pair, with an extended envelope but no significant rotation or velocity substructure.

quotes.quote: NGC4874: This galaxy has a large, extended envelope. It is the second brightest galaxy of the famous pair of cDs at the centre of the Coma cluster. The surface brightness profile was published by Peletier et al. \ (1990). No significant rotation or velocity substructure is detected for this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper links NGC 4874 to a strong concentration of Coma UCDs, matching central-giant velocities, and a high globular-cluster specific frequency.

quotes.quote: A strong concentration of confirmed and candidate UCDs is also seen around the cD galaxy NGC 4874. Fewer UCDs are found around NGC 4889, the other central giant, but this is possibly due to a gap in our ACS coverage at this location.
The central giants NGC 4874 and NGC 4889 have peculiar velocities of 7220 and 6495 km/s respectively.
Comparing to the spatial distribution of GCs from peng09 , we find a concentration of UCDs around NGC 4874 which also has a very high GC specific frequency.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4874 as the nearby cD galaxy whose halo could contain two candidates, but finds most detected PNe are intracluster rather than halo members.

quotes.quote: None of our candidates is close in space and velocity to any of these, but two PNe have measured velocities and positions that could be consistent with the cD envelope of NGC 4874, which is just outside our field, 120 from the field center. NGC 4874 itself has a radial velocity of 7224 and a velocity dispersion of 280 (Smith \ 2000). Most of our PNe, which have v =6100 and =760 , are therefore not associated with the halo of NGC 4874.

It is interesting that the measured mean and dispersion velocities for these ICPNe are smaller than those measured for the Coma cluster core centered around NGC 4874, v _ Coma =6853 , velocity dispersion _ Coma = 1082 (Colless Dunn, 1996).

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 4874 as a Coma central cD benchmark, noting its roughly 30,000 globular clusters, comparable BCG luminosity, and red-sequence extension among superluminous clusters.

quotes.quote: However, larger systems are known see harris_etal13 , such as the Coma central cD NGC 4874 that may hold as many as 30,000 GCs peng_etal11 .

The luminosities of these giant galaxies are comparable with or even higher than the two brightest BCGs whose GC systems have previously been studied to similar depth, namely NGC 4874 in Coma and NGC 4696 in Centaurus harris09a .

Notably, the same sort of red-sequence extension was also seen in the Hydra I BCG, NGC 3311 wehner_etal08 and in the Coma BCG NGC 4874, but interestingly, not in the other Coma supergiant NGC 4889 harris_etal09 .

sources.summary: The paper contrasts literature reports for NGC 4874 with MASSIVE data, noting earlier falling or weakly rising profiles but finding a substantial rise to at least 30 kpc.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, of the five galaxies with data in both this paper and loubseretal2008 (NGC 2832, NGC 3842, NGC 4839, NGC 4874, and NGC 4889), only NGC 3842 does not show a rising outer profile in our data.

Many galaxies in the Coma cluster have falling (R) thomasetal2007 , but that paper found all three Coma galaxies in the MASSIVE sample (NGC 4839, NGC 4874, and NGC 4889) to have at least some hint of a rising profile.
In our sample, the situation is reversed, with NGC 4839 measured to less than 10 kpc while NGC 4874 and NGC 4889 are measured to at least 30 kpc and show a substantial rise in .

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 4874 as its template field, showing that GC candidates dominate bright detections and illustrating the spatial distribution of its globular-cluster population.

quotes.quote: In all target fields, the overwhelming source of field contamination consists of small, faint background galaxies, whereas the GCs that are the objects of this study are unresolved or near-starlike.

The GC population now totally dominates the numbers of objects brighter than this level. Final lists of the photometry for each galaxy include (a) object positions (x, y on the reference image, and J2000 RA and Dec); (b) magnitude and uncertainty in each filter; and () DOLPHOT output parameters including , SNR, sharpness, roundness, and crowdedness in each filter.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4874 as a nearby cD galaxy offset from the Coma intracluster planetary-nebula velocity peak, arguing the diffuse-light ICPNs are not bound to it.

quotes.quote: Hence the ICPNs associated with the diffuse light at the position of the MSIS field are not bound to the nearby cD galaxy NGC 4874, whose radial velocity is 700 kms ^ 1 greater.

The galaxy NGC 4874, which lies nearer to the MSIS field, the peak of the X-ray emission, and the center of the larger-scale X-ray emission (White et al 1983), is at v_ sys = 7189 kms ^ 1 , displaced by 700 kms ^ 1 from the velocity peaks for both the galaxies and the ICPNs. Hence the ICPNs associated with the diffuse light at the position of the MSIS field are not bound to the nearby NGC 4874, but have motions similar to those of the Coma galaxies in this field.

gmw_00000580.name: MRC0114-211
gmw_00000580.overview: MRC0114-211 is a radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.41 [arXiv:1202.4489]. It is embedded within a complex large-scale structure, serving as a strong galaxy cluster candidate with a high density of surrounding red galaxies [arXiv:1202.4489].

sources.summary: The paper identifies MRC0114-211 as the strongest low-redshift cluster candidate in the radio-galaxy sample, with red IRAC sources concentrated around and near the HzRG.

quotes.quote: MRC0114-211 ( z=1.41 ) The environment of this radio galaxy had never been studied in the past. This field is the lowest redshift but also the strongest galaxy cluster candidate in the HzRG sample, overdense at a 6 level compared to SWIRE. Only 0.03 of the cells in our counts-in-cells analysis are found with such density. The distribution of the IRAC-selected red sources shows a clear spatial segregation.

The bright red galaxies lie preferentially in two N/S elongated structures with the radio galaxy embedded in the southern one. The northern clump is 2 ( 1  Mpc) north of the radio galaxy. Additional groups of red galaxies are also observed within 1  Mpc of the radio position, suggesting that MRC0114-211 is part of a complex large-scale structure.

gmw_00000581.name: And XXII
gmw_00000581.overview: Andromeda XXII is an ultra-faint dwarf spheroidal galaxy [arXiv:0909.0399, arXiv:2305.13360] that formed most of its stars in an early episode before z=5 [arXiv:2305.13360]. Located near the Triangulum Galaxy (M33), it is considered a likely satellite of M33 [arXiv:0909.0399, arXiv:1302.6590], though its exact gravitational association remains sensitive to orbital models [arXiv:2206.02801, arXiv:1807.05318].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Andromeda XXII as a faint new dwarf near M31/M33, likely in that satellite system and possibly the first Triangulum satellite, though its distance remains uncertain.

quotes.quote: We present the discovery of two new dwarf galaxies, Andromeda XXI and Andromeda XXII, located in the surroundings of the Andromeda and Triangulum galaxies (M31 and M33). These discoveries stem from the first year data of the Pan-Andromeda Archaeological Survey (PAndAS), a photometric survey of the M31/M33 group conducted with the Megaprime/MegaCam wide-field camera mounted on the Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope. Both satellites appear as spatial overdensities of stars which, when plotted in a color-magnitude diagram, follow metal-poor, =-1.8 , red giant branches at the distance of M31/M33. Andromeda XXI is a moderately bright dwarf galaxy ( M_V=-9.9 0.6 ), albeit with low surface brightness, emphasizing again that many relatively luminous M31 satellites still remain to be discovered. It is also a large satellite, with a half-light radius close to 1 , making it the fourth largest Local Group dwarf spheroidal galaxy after the recently discovered Andromeda XIX, Andromeda II and Sagittarius around the Milky Way, and supports the trend that M31 satellites are larger than their Milky Way counterparts. Andromeda XXII is much fainter ( M_V=-6.5 0.8 ) and lies a lot closer in projection to M33 than it does to M31 (42 vs. 224 ), suggesting that it could be the first Triangulum satellite to be discovered. Although this is a very exciting possibility in the context of a past interaction of M33 with M31 and the fate of its satellite system, a confirmation will have to await a good distance estimate to confirm its physical proximity to M33.

This limit to And XXII's distance clearly makes this dwarf galaxy a likely member of the M31/M33 satellite system. The g band LF of And XXII also shows a tentative detection of its horizontal branch, at a distance of 940 , very near the limit of the survey. Although this measurement is very uncertain, the absence of a clear horizontal branch at brighter magnitude would imply that And XXII is at least as distant as And XXI.

sources.summary: The paper revises And XXII's separation from M33 to 113 kpc, making its bound-satellite status sensitive to M33's orbital history and future proper-motion measurements.

quotes.quote: The most likely known dwarf galaxy associated with M33 is And  XXII as it has a close projected separation ( 43  kpc). However, the physical association between M33 and And  XXII ultimately depends on their deprojected distance. Using CFHT photometry, Chapman13 , estimated a separation of 59^ +21 _ 14  kpc between And  XXII and M33, and suggested that the two systems are currently gravitationally bound. Using our distances, we find a separation of 113.4^ +31.2 _ 29.7  kpc between these two galaxies.

While this is still within the estimated virial radius of M33 161 kpc, Patel17 , this new measurement makes the status of And  XXII as a bound satellite of M33 ultimately much more sensitive to the orbital history of the latter. Orbital solutions that exclude recent close encounters with M31 e.g., Patel17,VanderMarel19 , would be compatible with And  XXII being a current satellite of M33. However, models such as those put forward by McConnachie09 and Putman09 advocate for a recent <100  kpc pericenter passage of M33 around M31. They imply that And  XXII , if it ever was associated with M33, is now likely unbound from its former host.

sources.summary: The paper uses spectroscopy and photometry to show And XXII is a faint dSph with member stars, distance 853 kpc, low velocity dispersion, and likely M33-satellite status.

quotes.quote: The velocity distribution of stars in And XXII is shown in Figure  a22vels , highlighting the clear peak of 10 stars near 130 , representing \ (spectra are shown in Figure  a22spec ). The stars are also shown as a function of radius from the centre of the dwarf, with multiples of the half-light radius indicated. The probability that a given star is a member of \ is determined from a combination of its radial velocity, its distance from the RGB locus in the CMD, and its radial distance from the centre of .

The \ heliocentric velocity is v _r =-130.0 1.7 , +177 km/s relative to M31, but only +50 km/s relative to M33 (in agreement with Tollerud et al. \ 2012, v _r =-126.8 3.1 ). The dispersion is unresolved with 10 member stars at _v < 6.0 , 99.5 confidence. Using the photometry and spectroscopy of the confirmed member stars, we find a metallicity with median [ Fe/H ] =-1.6 0.1 . Our identified member stars in \ along with an assessment of the horizontal branch peak suggest a radial distance of 853 26 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper identifies XXII as an M31 ultra-faint dwarf and reports an early dominant star-formation episode before z=5, followed by about 35 percent later star formation.

quotes.quote: We select every galaxy targeted by the survey that has an absolute magnitude M_V>-7.0 , which leaves no ambiguity about their status as UFDs. We use absolute magnitudes from Savino22 , which are based on the PandAS structural parameters of Martin16 and are recalibrated on the basis of updated RR Lyrae distances. This selection yields six targets: XI ( M_V = -6.4 0.4 , 11 orbits of imaging), XII ( M_V = -6.6 0.5 , 11 orbits), XIII ( M_V = -6.8 0.4 , 9 orbits), XX ( M_V = -6.4 0.4 , 11 orbits), XXII ( M_V = -6.4 0.4 , 18 orbits), and XXVI ( M_V = -6.0^ +0.7 _ 0.5 , 16 orbits).

Figure fig:SFHs shows the lifetime SFHs for our six UFDs. Five of the UFDs in our sample ( XI , XII , XX , XXII and XXVI ) have a prominent episode of star formation in the earliest time bin ( t>12.6 Gyr), which drastically decreased in intensity by z 5 . Taken at face value, the best-fit SFHs indicate that all five galaxies formed 50 of their stellar mass in the oldest time bin. This initial strong episode of star formation is followed by varying degrees of star formation at later times ranging from 15 ( XI ) to 35 ( XXII ) of the total stellar mass.

sources.summary: The paper tests whether And XXII is a satellite of M33 rather than M31, finding its orbit is not independently moving along the same path as M33.

quotes.quote: Observations also show ample evidence for group infall; dejong2010 recently presented evidence that Leo IV and Leo V could be a bound ``tumbling pair" falling into the Milky Way and, in their discovery of And XXII, martin2009 noted that it lies closer in projection to M33 than M31 and suggested that it could be a satellite of M33.

First we consider whether And XXII ( a = 267 9 kpc, e = 0.20 0.06 ) might be not a satellite of M31 but of M33, which itself has parameters a = 206 9 kpc, e = 0.27 0.08 . tollerud2012 and chapman2012 both found radial velocities consistent with this hypothesis. The latter authors analysed N-body simulations of the M31-M33 system, which suggest that And XXII may indeed be a satellite of M33. We find that the semi-major axes are not similar, from which we conclude that the two satellites are not moving independently along the same orbit; but whether this means they are orbiting each other or are unrelated, we cannot determine from this analysis.

sources.summary: The paper presents And XXII as the only potential M33 satellite, noting that its location, velocity, and distance favor M33 over M31 but proper motion is needed to determine bound status.

quotes.quote: And XXII lies to the southwest of M33 on the sky and its relative systemic velocity, angular separation from M33, and distance all indicate that it may be a satellite of M33 instead of M31 tollerud12, chapman13, martin09 .

Assuming that bound satellites follow the dark matter distribution of their host, And XXII would remain bound to M33 during such an encounter based on these simulations. These results strongly depend on the adopted M33 orbital history and other potential orbits may not yield the same conclusion for And XXII.

To date, there is only one potential M33 candidate satellite, And XXII, but it is unclear based on its one-dimensional kinematics whether And XXII is bound to M31 or M33. A proper motion measurement for And XXII would make it possible to derive a full orbital history in the combined M31+M33 gravitational potential and possibly determine if it is a true satellite of M33 or just another member of the larger M31 system.
