The primary model parameters are listed in Table 1. cccccccc Ht Model Parameters m & & v & & r_ in & r_ out & y_ in & y_ out 1.1 &0.429 &1.414 &0.05 &1.61 R_g &120 R_g &2.04 &0.028 The electron scattering optical depth measured inward from the outer edge of the spherical region, ( ) , is computed using Equation ( eq:tau ), and plotted in Figure fig:tau . We note that the optical depth down to dimensionless radius = /R_g = 1.86 is close to unity, which is required in order to ensure that the relativistically blurred iron line emission near the black hole can be resolved spectroscopically, in reasonable agreement with the conclusions of (Wilkins et al. 2014). We find that = 1.08 at =r_ ISCO , and decreases monotonically out to = 0 at =120 R_g . Simulated Time Lags Since the eigenvalues, _n , in our calculation are functions of the Fourier frequency, _F , we must select a sample of discrete Fourier frequencies and evaluate the time lags at those frequencies. The reader should keep in mind that a continuous range of Fourier frequencies is not required in our model, since we do not need to perform the inverse Fourier transformation. Instead, the required time lags are computed directly from knowledge of the Fourier transform itself. In our application to 1H 0707-495, we select values for the vector of Fourier frequencies, _F (in Hz), that are comparable to the values used by Fabian et al. (2009) and Zoghbi et al. (2010) in their plots of the time lags. The 12 values we adopt for the Fourier frequency are: _F= 7.94 10^ 5 , 1.58 10^ 4 , 2.51 10^ 4 , 3.98 10^ 4 , 6.31 10^ 4 , 1.00 10^ 3 , 1.58 10^ 3 , 2.24 10^ 3 , 3.31 10^ 3 , 5.01 10^ 3 , 7.08 10^ 3 , 1.00 10^ 2 . The 12 corresponding values for the dimensionless Fourier frequency, , computed using Equation ( eq:hatomega ) are: = 0.021, 0.042, 0.066, 0.104, 0.166, 0.262, 0.416, 0.587, 0.869, 1.32, 1.86, 2.62. We note that the value of the photon injection number N_0 has no affect on the time lags, and we can set the injection time t_0 = q_0 = 0 without loss of generality. The first 10 complex eigenvalues, _n , obtained for each discrete value of the Fourier frequency adopted here are plotted in Figure fig:AGN2 , with the value of increasing from bottom to top. The eigenvalues are indicated by the points, and the colored lines are added to clarify the eigenvalue sequence for each frequency. As noted in Section sec:compute , the computation of the simulated time lags, t , using our theoretical model also requires specification of the injection radius r_0 , the photon injection energy _0 , the electron temperature = k T_e/(m_e ^2) , and the hard and soft channel energies, _h and _s , respectively. We set the value of the source energy using _0=0.89 keV, which notably correlates with the broadened and skewed energy of the iron L-line emission that Fabian et al. (2009) reported for 1H 0707-495. We set the value of the injection radius using r_0=16R_g , which yields _0=0.206 according to Equation ( eq:nondim1 ). The value adopted for the dimensionless electron temperature is = 0.05 , so that k T_e = 25.6 keV. This yields for the electron temperature T_e = 2.96 10^8 K, which is consistent with the expected temperature range for the corona (e.g., Wilkins et al. 2014; Kara et al. 2017). A remaining issue is how we set the soft and hard channel energies in our model, _s and _h , respectively. In their time lag computations, Fabian et al. (2009) use _s = 0.3-1 keV for the soft energy band and _h = 1-4 keV for the hard energy band. However, our model is not able to accommodate an energy range for the soft and hard bands, and instead we need to specify exact values for _s and _h . We set our hard and soft channel energies to the values _s = 0.33 keV and _h = 1.76 keV in order to approximate the variability found in these energy bands. We have analyzed the convergence of the results obtained for the time lags as a function of the value of the maximum index, N_ max , appearing in the sum in Equation ( eq:finalF ). We find that setting N_ max = 5 is sufficient to ensure convergence to a relative error of 1 , and therefore we generally adopt the value N_ max = 10 as a conservative value when computing the time lags for each Fourier frequency . The resulting time lag distribution, t , computed using Equation ( eq:lag ), is plotted as a function of the Fourier frequency _F = /(2 ) in Figure fig:AGN3 , and compared with the observational data for 1H 0707-495 reported by Fabian et al. (2009) and Zoghbi et al. (2010).

sources.summary: The paper finds 1H 0707--495 has comparable intrinsic lags to IRAS 13224--3809 but, given its larger mass, a smaller, dynamic extended corona.

quotes.quote: With the larger mass and the comparable intrinsic lags, the coronal height in 1H 0707--495 should be smaller than that in IRAS 13224--3809. investigated the emissivity profiles using various coronal geometries and suggested a presence of the 1H 0707--495 corona at 2 r_ g extending radially outwards to 30 r_ g . Recent studies on the relativistic reflection spectra of 1H 0707--495 suggested that its corona should be very compact and located at h 3 r_ g with an extended size of 1 r_ g Szanecki2020 .

Regarding this, proposed that the 1H 0707--495 corona may locate at 2-5 r_ g and its size may extend outwards to 20 r_ g during its maximum flux. Recently, showed that the frequency-dependent time lags of 1H 0707--495 could be explained using a dual lamp-post model. They fixed the lower source height at 2 r_ g and found the upper coronal height varying at 3 - 20 r_ g . Our finding also supports that the 1H 0707--495 corona is dynamic and extended as in IRAS 13224--3809, but with smaller physical distance due to their mass difference.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes a long XMM-Newton observation of the highly variable NLS1 galaxy 1H0707-495, interpreting its spectral drop and soft excess as broad iron lines rather than absorption.

quotes.quote: A detailed analysis of a long XMM-Newton observation of the Narrow Line Seyfert 1 galaxy 1H0707-495 is presented, including spectral fitting, spectral variability and timing studies. The two main features in the spectrum are the drop at 7 and a complex excess below 1 . These are well described by two broad, K and L, iron lines. Alternative models based on absorption, although they may fit the high energy drop, cannot account for the 1 complexity and the spectrum as a whole.

1H0707-495 ( z=0.0411 , hereafter 1H0707), is a highly variable Narrow Line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) galaxy ( 1999ApJS..125..297L ). It has a steep spectrum and shows a strong soft excess, and although these are common among other NLS1, they are extreme in 1H0707 ( 1999MNRAS.309..113V ).

sources.summary: The paper models X-ray time lags in 1H 0707--495 and argues they are consistent with reverberation from scattering through partially covering circumnuclear absorbing material.

quotes.quote: The narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy 1H 0707--495 has previously been identified as showing time lags between flux variations in the soft- (0.3-1 keV) and medium-energy (1-4 keV) X-ray bands that oscillate between positive and negative values as a function of the frequency of the mode of variation. Here we measure and analyse the lags also between a harder X-ray band (4-7.5 keV) and the soft and medium bands, using existing XMM-Newton data, and demonstrate that the entire spectrum of lags, considering both the full energy range, 0.3-7.5 keV, and the full frequency range, 10^ 5 10^ 2 Hz, are inconsistent with previous claims of arising as reverberation associated with the inner accretion disk. Instead we demonstrate that a simple reverberation model, in which scattering or reflection is present in all X-ray bands, explains the full set of lags without requiring any ad hoc explanation for the time lag sign changes.

The range of time delays required to explain the observed lags extends up to about 1800 s in the hard band. The results are consistent with reverberation caused by scattering of X-rays passing through an absorbing medium whose opacity decreases with increasing energy and that partially-covers the source. A high covering factor of absorbing and scattering circumnuclear material is inferred.

sources.summary: The paper compares 1H0707-495 as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 with reverberation delays, a strong soft excess, and time lags tied to reprocessed reflected emission.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage 2011 In previous work, we discussed the detection of reverberation delays in the NLS1 1H0707-495. The delays originate close to the black hole.

One other object, 1H0707-495, also shows a strong soft excess below 1 keV. It was shown recently that because of the high iron abundance, its soft excess is mainly due to a strong iron L emission ( 2009Natur.459..540F,2010MNRAS.401.2419Z ) produced by partially ionised reflection ( 2005MNRAS.358..211R,1991MNRAS.249..352G ).

1H0707-495 also shows a time delay between the hard band (1.0--4.0 keV) dominated by the direct power-law emission, and the soft band (0.3--1.0 keV) dominated by the reflected emission ( 2009Natur.459..540F,2010MNRAS.401.2419Z ). The soft lag is the first to be detected, and is a confirmation that the soft band is dominated by reprocessed emission.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1H 0707-495 as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy in an unprecedented low soft-X-ray state, modeled with relativistically blurred reflection near a rapidly spinning black hole.

quotes.quote: The Narrow Line Seyfert 1 Galaxy 1H 0707-495 went in to a low state from 2010 December to 2011 February, discovered by a monitoring campaign using the X-Ray Telescope on the Swift satellite. We triggered a 100 ks XMM-Newton observation of the source in 2011 January, revealing the source to have dropped by a factor of ten in the soft band, below 1 keV, and a factor of 2 at 5 keV, compared with a long observation in 2008. The sharp spectral drop in the source usually seen around 7 keV now extends to lower energies, below 6 keV in our frame. The 2011 spectrum is well fit by a relativistically-blurred reflection spectrum similar to that which fits the 2008 data, except that the emission is now concentrated solely to the central part of the accretion disc.

The Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 Galaxy 1H 0707-495 is bright in soft X-rays where it shows the steep spectrum and rapid variability typical of its class. XMM-Newton observations revealed a sharp drop in its spectrum around 7 keV, which can be interpreted either as an iron absorption feature or as the blue wing of a large broad emission line (Boller et al 2002; Fabian et al 2002).

sources.summary: The paper uses the reverberation lag spectrum of the narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy 1H 0707-495 to infer an extended, low-height X-ray source above its accretion disc.

quotes.quote: We find that the observed lag spectrum of the narrow line Seyfert 1 galaxy 1H 0707-495 implies an X-ray source extending radially outwards to around 35 \ and at a height of around 2 \ above the plane of the accretion disc, consistent with the constraints obtained independently by considering the emissivity profile of the accretion disc.

The narrow line Seyfert 1 galaxy 1H 0707-495 was the first AGN found to exhibit reverberation lags fabian+09 , where the variability in the spectral band dominated by reflection from the accretion disc is found to lag behind that in the spectral band corresponding to direct emission from the coronal X-ray source by tens to hundreds of seconds zoghbi+09 .

The lag spectrum of 1H 0707-495 was first obtained from a 500 \ lightcurve by zoghbi+09 and then in more detail following observations with XMM Newton totalling 1.3 \ by kara+12 . Such a lag spectrum, from a 1 \ lag spectrum of 1H 0707-495 between the `hard' (1.0-4.0 ) band dominated by the primary continuum and the `soft' (0.3-1.0 ) band dominated by reflection from the accretion disc is shown in Fig. lagspec_1h0707.fig . At temporal frequencies of around 10^ 3 , a lag of around 30 \ can be seen between the arrival of the X-ray continuum and the reflection from the accretion disc. Na i vely, this can be converted into the distance of the reflecting material from the primary X-ray source, which taking the mass of the black hole in 1H 0707-495 to be 2 10^6 , zhou_wang , gives a distance of 2 \ (a gravitational radius, 1 \ = GM ^2 ).

sources.summary: The paper argues that reflection-based models for 1H 0707-495 imply extreme super-Eddington accretion and a clumpy wind, which may undermine its high-spin interpretation.

quotes.quote: Relativistic reflection models of the X-ray spectrum of the `complex' Narrow Line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) 1H 0707-495 require a high spin, moderate inclination, low mass black hole. With these parameters fixed, the observed optical/UV emission directly determines the mass accretion rate through the outer disc and hence predicts the bolometric luminosity. This is 140-260 the Eddington limit.

Changing inclination angle with respect to a clumpy wind structure gives a possible explanation for the otherwise puzzling difference between `complex' NLS1 such as 1H 0707-495 and `simple' ones like PG 1244+026. Lines of sight which intercept the wind show deep absorption features at iron from the hot phase of the wind, together with stochastic dips and complex absorption when the clumps occult the X-ray source (complex NLS1), whereas both these features are absent for more face-on inclination (simple NLS1). This geometry is quite different to the clean view of a flat disc which is assumed for the spin measurements in relativistic reflection models, so it is possible that even 1H 0707-495 has low spin.

sources.summary: The paper uses 1H 0707 495 as an NLS1 case where XMM-Newton emissivity profiles imply a low-height corona extending over the disc and expanding during high X-ray flux.

quotes.quote: Through this technique, 1h0707_emis_paper find that the emissivity profile of the disc in the NLS1 galaxy 1H 0707 495 approximately takes the form of a twice broken power law, falling off steeply with index >7 over the inner regions of the disc, then flattening to almost a constant between 5 35 \ before falling off slightly steeper than ^ 3 over the outer part of the disc.

The emissivity profile of the accretion disc in 1H 0707 495 is best explained by illumination of the disc by a corona extended radially over the disc to around 35 , but at a low height from the surface of the disc.

Between early observations in 2002 and 2011, the X-ray flux received from the NLS1 galaxy 1H 0707 495 remained at a fairly consistent mean level, although frequently varying around this by factors of two or three over timescales of just hours. 1h0707_var_paper divided up more than 1.3 \ of observations of 1H 0707 495 made with XMM-Newton into periods of high and low flux. When the X-ray luminosity was greater, the corona was, on average, found to extend over a larger portion of the inner disc. The outer break radius in the emissivity profile from the flattened middle section to the inverse-cube fall-off over the outer disc was found to increase from 23_ 3 ^ +3 \ during the lowest flux intervals to 30_ 3 ^ +5 during the highest.

sources.summary: The paper reports a possible 3800 s X-ray QPO in the NLS1 galaxy 1H 0707-495, estimates a 5.2 million solar-mass black hole, and argues the transient signal follows the QPO-mass relation.

quotes.quote: Here we report on the detection of a possible X-ray QPO signal with a period of 3800 s at a confidence level >99.99 in the narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy (NLS1) 1H 0707-495 in one data set in 0.2-10 keV taken with XMM-Newton . The statistical significance is higher than that of most previously reported QPOs in AGNs. The QPO is highly coherent (quality factor Q= / 15 ) with a high rms fractional variability ( 15 ). A comprehensive analysis of the optical spectra of this AGN is also performed, yielding a central BH mass 5.2 10^6 M_ from the broad emission lines based on the scaling relation.

The QPO follows closely the known frequency-BH mass relation, which spans from stellar-mass to supermassive BHs. The absence of the QPO in other observations of the object suggests it a transient phenomenon.

sources.summary: The paper models 1H 0707-495 soft-band PSDs, finding poorer BPL fits than for Mrk 766 and noting it among sources where non-stationary variable PSDs may remain possible.

quotes.quote: fig:BestFitExamples show the best-fit BPL models to the soft-band PSDs of 1H 0707-495 and Mrk 766 (top and bottom panels, respectively). The lower plots, attached to each panel, show the best-fitting residual at each Fourier frequency, _j , i.e. \ [ P _ best-fit ( _j; )-P_ obs ( _j) ] / P_ obs ( _j) in which P_ obs ( _j) is the standard deviation of the average periodograms. According to the resulting ^2 values, the BPL model fits well the observed power spectrum of Mrk 766, but less so the 1H 0707-495 PSD (the null hypothesis probability, p_ null , is less than 2.2 per cent in this source).

The right-hand panels in Fig. fig:BestFitExamples show the best-fitting BPLGRE models to the soft-band PSDs of 1H 0707-495 and Mrk 766 (upper and lower panels, respectively). The residual plots of the BPL (shown in the left panels) and BPLGRE models are very similar for both sources.

On the other hand, non-stationary, variable PSDs may still be possible in the case of SWIFT J2127+5654, NGC 3516, and 1H 0707-495, which show the largest BPL best-fit ^2 values among the sources (NGC 7314 and Ark 564, in the case of the iron-line band PSDs).

sources.summary: The paper uses 1H 0707-495 as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 case study, emphasizing its rapid X-ray variability, reverberation soft lag, inferred coronal height, and low black-hole spin fit.

quotes.quote: 1H 0707-495 (z=0.0411) is a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy with a BH mass estimate of M =2 10^6 M _ . It is one of the most highly variable Seyfert galaxies, commonly known to increase by an order of magnitude in flux on timescales as short as one hour. Therefore this makes this source an ideal target for extensive spectral-timing analysis as previous studies based on the X-ray data from the XMM-Newton satellite have shown so far.

and showed that the high-frequency variations in the soft excess from 0.3-1 keV lag behind those in the 1-4 keV band by 30 s. They interpreted this short timescale lag as the reverberation time delay between the primary-emitting corona and the inner accretion disc. This short time delay would put the corona at a height of <10 _ g from the accretion disc (using the aforementioned value for the BH mass).

sources.summary: The paper uses 1H 0707--495 as the key extreme AGN case whose short Fe-K reverberation lags and broad spectral features may arise from winds.

quotes.quote: This kind of lag was first significantly detected in XMM-Newton observations of 1H 0707--495 fab09 , in which the light curve of the fast variability in the soft X-ray band (0.3--1 keV) was lagged by 30 s behind the same variability seen in a hard band (1--4 keV). This corresponds to 3 R_g/ for the black-hole mass of 1H 0707--495 ( M_ BH =2 10^6M_ ; zho05 ), where R_g=GM_ BH /^2 and M_ BH is the black hole mass. Lags were also detected in this object in the energy band containing the Fe-K line kar13b , such that photons in the Fe-K energy band lag behind those in the adjacent energy bands by 50 s, corresponding to 5 R_g/ . The full lag-energy plot for the fast variability in 1H 0707--495 shows that the lags associated with the Fe-K line emission are significant across the entire 5--7 keV band, so this is a broad feature.

This is especially attractive for 1H 0707--495 as its multi-wavelength spectrum clearly implies that the accretion flow is super-Eddington don16 . This object should power strong winds, and emission/absorption from this wind can fit the strong and broad Fe-K emission features observed in the spectrum without requiring extreme relativistic effects and extreme super-solar iron abundances hag16 .

sources.summary: The review describes h07 as an X-ray-weak NLS1 whose variable spectra and emissivity profile indicate a corona that contracts in low states and expands in high states.

quotes.quote: A breathing corona in h07 h07 \ has been observed over a dozen times since the launch of . The NLS1 varies significantly in the X-rays and modestly in the UV (e.g. ) and always appears X-ray weak ( , ). That is to say the X-rays are dim compared to its UV luminosity (e.g. ).

As the X-ray flux varies by about a factor of ten, the spectrum and \ line profile change significantly. Analysis of the emissivity profile in various flux states infers changes in the spatial extent of the X-ray source ( ). The corona apparently contracts when the luminosity is low and expands when it is high.

sources.summary: The paper models 1H 0707--495 Fe-K lags with a highly ionised wind, interpreting its strong P-Cygni-like profile as a high-inclination, large-solid-angle wind.

quotes.quote: This broad iron line feature in the lag spectrum is most significant in super-Eddington sources such as Ark 564 ( L/L_ Edd 1 ) and 1H 0707--495 ( L/L_ Edd 10 ). The observed lag timescales correspond to very short distances of several R_g/ , so that they have been used to argue for extremely small `lamp-post' coronae close to the event horizon of rapidly spinning black holes. Here we show for the first time that these Fe-K short lags are more likely to arise from scattering in a highly-ionised wind, launched at 50 R_g , rotating and outflowing with a typical velocity of 0.2c . We show that this model can simultaneously fit the time-averaged energy spectra and the short-timescale lag-energy spectra of both 1H 0707--495 and Ark 564.

The Fe-K line in 1H 0707--495 has a strong P-Cygni-like profile, which requires that the wind solid angle is large and that our line of sight intercepts the wind. By contrast the lack of an absorption line in the energy spectrum of Ark 564 requires rather face-on geometry, while the weaker broad Fe-K emission in the energy and lag-energy spectra argue for a smaller solid angle of the wind. This is consistent with theoretical predictions that the winds get stronger when the sources are more super-Eddington, supporting the idea of AGN feedback via radiation pressure driven winds.

In Ark 564 our line of sight does not intercept the wind, while in 1H 0707--495 our line of sight does intercept the wind, resulting in a distinct blueshifted absorption line. There is also a noticeable difference that the broad iron emission line feature is stronger in 1H 0707--495, which we interpret as being due to a larger wind solid angle caused by the higher super-Eddington luminosity.

sources.summary: The paper models 1H 0707-495 as a Narrow Line Seyfert 1 galaxy whose shifted X-ray spectral drop is explained by relativistically blurred ionized reflection from its accretion disc.

quotes.quote: The first (hereafter GT for Guaranteed Time) XMM-Newton X-ray spectrum of the Narrow Line Seyfert 1 galaxy, 1H 0707-495 ( z=0.0411 ), showed a dramatic spectral drop at 7 keV (Boller et al 2002). This was interpreted by the authors as due to photoelectric absorption by neutral iron close to the source. The lack of any iron L-shell absorption in the object, which has a strong soft X-ray excess, implies that the absorber only partially cover the source.

If the reflecting material is the inner parts of an accretion disc then the reflection spectrum is blurred by Doppler and gravitational redshift effects. The sharp 7 keV drop in 1H 0707-495 is then the blue edge of the relativistically-blurred iron emission line. The shift in energy of the edge during the second XMM-Newton observation can then be due to a change in ionisation or geometry of the irradiated material.

sources.summary: For 1H 0707--495, the paper reports high-frequency soft-band lags and favors large-scale reverberation by circumnuclear scattering material over an inner-disk reflection interpretation.

quotes.quote: One noticeable feature of the lag spectrum of 1H 0707--495 is that the lags are significantly negative at high frequency : i.e. the soft band lags the hard band, contrary to the expectation outlined above. This has been interpreted as evidence for extreme inner-disk reflection from the accretion disk. A model describing such lags ascribes the lagging of the soft band to two effects, both of which are required: (i) a factor > 9 overabundance of Fe; and (ii) amplification of the Fe disk reflection line by the process of ``light-bending'', in which light rays near the black hole are preferentially bent onto the accretion disk, which then produces stronger-than-expected reflection .

One NLS1, 1H 0707--495, displays soft band lags at high frequencies, which we interpret as being due to scattered light present at all photon energies in this source. An alternative model ascribing the soft-band lags to inner disk reflection is strongly disfavoured by consideration of the full energy dependence.

sources.summary: The paper compares IRAS 13224-3809 with 1H 0707-495, noting its extreme NLS1 behavior, broad iron features, soft lag, and very compact X-ray source.

quotes.quote: The most extreme such objects are 1H 0707-495 and IRAS13228-3809, which both show a sharp drop above 7 keV in XMM Newton spectra (Boller et al 2002, 2003). 1H 0707-495 has been further studied several times with XMM, including a long 500 ks dataset in 2008 which revealed broad iron K and L lines and a soft lag of about 30 s (Fabian et al 2009; Zoghbi et al 2010, 2011).

The unusual spectrum and 7 keV drop of both objects have been interpreted as due to either intervening absorption or strong relativistic blurring of a reflection component (Boller et al 2002, 2003; Fabian et al 2004). 1H 0707-495 dropped into a low state for about 2 months at the start of 2011 during which an XMM spectrum showed evidence for even more blurring. The results are consistent with the power-law component of the X-ray source lying within one gravitational radius of the central black hole (Fabian et al 2012).

sources.summary: The paper uses 1H0707--495 as an AGN comparison object, discussing its soft lags, reflection interpretation, negative-lag frequency, and scaling problems relative to stellar-mass black-hole QPOs.

quotes.quote: zoghbi01 proposed that the 30 s soft time lags seen in the active galactic nucleus 1H0707--495 at a Fourier frequency of 5 10^ 4 Hz are due to reflection of photons from the corona around the central black hole off the accretion disc. Given the steep emissivity index inferred from the spectral fits to the time averaged spectra of this fabian and other objects e.g., miller2009,chiang,risaliti , the bulk of the reflection must take place very close to the inner edge of the accretion disc.

In the case of 1H0707--495 the lags become negative at 5 10^ 4 Hz; if the frequency at which the lags become negative is proportional to mass (as in the case of lags produced at the inner edge of a Keplerian disc), this frequency would be 250 Hz for a 10- black hole. This value is consistent with the frequency range of the HFQPOs in GRO J1655--40 and XTE J1550--56, but the time lags have the opposite sign. Furthermore, the magnitude of the time lags of the HFQPOs in these four systems is one to two orders of magnitude larger than the lags of 1H0707--495 scaled down by the black-hole mass-ratio.

sources.summary: The paper cites 1H0707-495 as an AGN with very high reflection-derived spin, links its low inner-disc ionization to a dense thin disc, and says its extended corona still leaves the spin measurement robust.

quotes.quote: Spin determinations of AGN using X-ray reflection have been ongoing since Dabrowski et al (1997) with recent results including MCG--6-30-15 ( a=cJ/ GM^2=0.989^ +0.009 _ 0.002 , Brenneman Reynolds 2006), 1H0707-495 ( a>0.98 , Fabian et al 2009); IRAS13224-3809 ( a=0.989 0.001 , Fabian et al 2013a) (see recent reviews by Reynolds 2013 and Brenneman 2013 for summaries of more AGN spin results). The high statistical precision of the above three results is due to a combination of low coronal height and high iron abundance, which emphasizes features in the reflection spectrum.
The low ionization parameter often found with the reflection method for the inner disc of AGN (e.g. < 20 for 1H0707-495 and IRAS13224-3809) indicates that the disc is dense and thus probably thin.

On the basis of the emissivity profile, Wilkins Fabian (2012) show that the corona in 1H0707-495 is extended. There is however significant reflection from the inner disc so the spin measurement is robust.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes XMM-Newton spectra of the NLS1 galaxy 1H 0707 495, finding luminosity-linked coronal expansion, X-ray continuum softening, and reflection behavior consistent with an expanding lower-density corona.

quotes.quote: The X-ray spectra of the narrow line Seyfert 1 galaxy, 1H 0707 495, obtained with XMM-Newton , from time periods of varying X-ray luminosity are analysed in the context of understanding the changes to the X-ray emitting corona that lead to the extreme variability seen in the X-ray emission from active galactic nuclei (AGN). The emissivity profile of the accretion disc, illuminated by the X-ray emitting corona, along with previous measurements of reverberation time lags are used to infer the spatial extent of the X-ray source. By fitting a twice-broken power law emissivity profile to the relativistically-broadened iron K fluorescence line, it is inferred that the X-ray emitting corona expands radially, over the plane of the accretion disc, by 25 to 30 per cent as the luminosity increases, contracting again as the luminosity decreases, while increases in the measured reverberation lag as the luminosity increases would require also variation in the vertical extent of the source above the disc. The spectrum of the X-ray continuum is found to soften as the total X-ray luminosity increases and we explore the variation in reflected flux as a function of directly-observed continuum flux. These three observations combined with simple, first-principles models constructed from ray tracing simulations of extended coron \ self-consistently portray an expanding corona whose average energy density decreases, but with a greater number of scattering particles as the luminosity of this extreme object increases.

sources.summary: The paper models 1H0707--495, a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy, explaining its X-ray spectrum and rapid spectral variation with a variable double partial covering model.

quotes.quote: The Narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy 1H0707--495 is known to exhibit significant X-ray spectral variations. Its X-ray energy spectrum is characterized by a strong soft excess emission, an extremely deep iron K-edge structure at 7 keV, and a putative iron L-line/edge feature at 1 keV. We have found that the energy spectrum of 1H0707--495 in 0.5--10 keV is successfully explained by a ``variable double partial covering model'' where the original continuum spectrum, which is composed of the soft multi-color disk blackbody component and the hard power-law component, is partially covered by two ionized absorption layers with different ionization states and the same partial covering fraction. The lower-ionized and thicker absorption layer primarily explains the iron K-edge feature, and the higher-ionized and thinner absorption layer explains the L-edge feature.

We propose that the observed spectral variation of 1H0707--495 is caused by three physically independent variations with different timescales; (1) intrinsic luminosity variation over days, (2) variation of partial covering fraction at a timescale of hours, and (3) small intrinsic hard component variation above 3 keV in a timescale of hours or less.

sources.summary: The paper treats 1H 0707-495 as a strong relativistic-reflection source whose XMM data show iron features, reverberation lags, and two reflection components.

quotes.quote: 1H 0707-495: This is a well known source which displays strong evidence of prominent relativistic reflection. A 500 ks observation with XMM-Newton in 2008 revealed the presence of both iron K and L emission lines, and a reverberation lag of around 30 s between the continuum and reflection emission. A more detailed examination of this lag by Kara13_1h0707 , using over 1 Ms of data, found evidence of an iron K line in the lag-energy spectrum. In general, the spectral shape and variability of 1H 0707-495 can be modelled with a power law continuum plus two separate relativistic reflection components, with the same blurring parameters but different ionisation states Zoghbi10, Dauser12, Fabian12 . This is suggested to correspond to regions of different density on the surface of the disk, potentially caused by turbulence.

sources.summary: The paper treats 1H0707-495 as a complex NLS1 with strong Fe K variability, a very short reverberation lag, and a mass comparable to PG1244+026.

quotes.quote: Complex narrow line Seyfert 1s (NLS1s), such as 1H0707-495, differ from simple NLS1s like PG1244+026 by showing stronger broad spectral features at Fe K and larger amplitude flux variability.

Detection of a very short lag time ( 30  s) in the complex NLS1 1H0707-495 is taken as unequivocal support for the high spin relativistic reflection picture, as this implies distances of the source from the disc of <2R_g for a 3 10^6M_ black hole.

Leighly (1999) give the FWHM for the H line widths in PG1244+026 and 1H0707-495 as 830 and 1050 km/s, respectively. These are corrected for FeII and have the narrow H component subtracted assuming that this is 0.1 the [ OIII ] line intensity (see also Leighly Moore 2004). Both sources have intrinsic optical luminosities which are very similar, so these give masses which are 2.4 10^6 and 3.7 10^6M_ , respectively (Nikolajuk et al 2009).

sources.summary: The paper presents 1H 0707--495 time-lag and coherence results, noting it is fainter than Ark 564 and NGC 4051 but more variable than Mrk 335.

quotes.quote: Figure fig2 shows the same results for 1H 0707--495 and Mrk 335 (two sources that are fainter, and, in the case of Mrk 355, less variable). They were calculated using equations eq3 and eq1 , respectively. The coherence estimates decrease with increasing frequency in all cases.

For example, despite the fact that the mean count rate of the 0.3-1 and 2-4 keV light curves is significantly higher in the case of Mrk 335, _ crit , 1H 0707--495 > _ crit , Mrk 335 . This is because 1H 0707--495 is much more variable.

sources.summary: The paper uses 1H 0707--495 as an observed NLS1 example whose emissivity profile implies a radially structured corona that changes between bright and dim states.

quotes.quote: For the narrow line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) galaxy 1H 0707--495 the emissivity profile was consistent with a twice-broken power law. The profile was steep in the inner region where light bending is dominant, flat at intermediate radii where the corona blankets the disc, and drops with ^ 3 at large distances. Further analysis shows the emissivity profile, and hence the geometry of the corona, may change depending on the flux of the source. For example, found the corona in 1H 0707--495 to be extended in the bright state but compact when the source was dim, showing a dynamic nature of the corona undergoing phase changes and altering its geometry significantly during these processes. The emissivity profile is a strong probe for geometry of the corona that can be used alongside various other tools including the reflection fraction (e.g. WilkinsANProc2015,Wilkins2015a ) and time-lag analysis (e.g. Wilkins2013 ) in order to gain further insights into the nature of the X-ray source.

sources.summary: The paper fits 1H0707--495 with a spherical corona model, noting its complex corona hints, wavy lag residuals, good lag fit, and literature-consistent constrained mass.

quotes.quote: Our sample consists of 1H0707--495, Ark 564, NGC 4051 and IRAS 13224--3809, all of which have shown hints of possessing a complex corona or having more than one variability component. found some wavy-residuals in the lag-frequency spectra of 1H0707--495 and Ark 564 which are not well-fitted by the KYNREFREV model, the public reverberation model that assumes a lamp-post geometry.

We find that the spherical corona model provides statistically good fits to all AGN investigated here. The constrained mass is consistent with those reported in the literature e.g., for 1H0707--495, Ark 564, NGC 4051 and IRAS 13224--3809, respectively Zhou2005,Botte2004,Emmanoulopoulos2014,Chainakun2016 .
Although there is possibility that some AGN such as 1H0707--495 may have a low black hole spin Done2016 , the effects of spin and inclination on the time lags should be weak.

sources.summary: J0708 is treated as a well-known complex NLS1 whose spectrum has a poor simple power-law fit, sharp drops, broad iron lines, and multi-component reflection models.

quotes.quote: J0708 (1H 0707-495) J0708 is a well-known C-NLS1s. The spectral fitting in the range of 2-12 keV gives a ^2_ = 1.83, indicating that a simple power-law model is not a good representation of the data. Indeed, its X-ray spectrum exhibits evident complex features, such as a sharp drop around 7 keV at the rest frame Boller2002 and/or broad iron K and L emission lines Fabian2009 , which are usually explained as a relativistically blurred reflection from an iron-rich accretion disk. The XMM-Newton EPIC-PN time-integrated spectrum of J0708 in 0.3-10.0 keV had been modeled by Kara2013 in detail, including components: a Galactic absorption, an intrinsic absorption, a blackbody component, two power-law continua, a relativistically blurred component, and two reflectors irradiated by two power-law components. The hard power-law component is originated from a region close to the center with short time-scale variability, and the soft power-law component is produced in an extended region with long variation. Both power-law continua likely contribute to the reflection.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes 1H 0707-495 with over 1 Ms of XMM-Newton exposure and finds three hard-energy QPO candidates near 1-2 x 10^-4 Hz.

quotes.quote: 1H 0707-495 1H 0707-495 has been studied extensively by XMM-Newton with a total observing time in excess of 1 Ms (e.g. Kara20131H1Msdata ). Initial plc search As shown in Figure fig:1H_multiplot , we detect 3 QPO candidates in three observations (including one borderline detection), from a total of 11, with high fractional rms ( > 20 ) and across a narrow frequency range of 1-2 10 ^ 4 Hz. We find a borderline 3 QPO candidate in OBSID:0653510601, although in this case, the QPO candidate frequency lies close to or even at the white noise cutoff in some energy bins. For this observation, we find a slight shift towards lower frequencies, but there is minimal change in the fractional-rms of the QPO candidate between each of these observations. We find the peak QPO candidate significance in OBSID:0653510301 and 0653510501 to lie in the 4 - 6 keV range, with no 3 energy bands extending below 3.8 keV, whilst we find only borderline 3 features in OBSID:0653510601 in the 4 - 8 keV energy range.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1H 0707--495 as a local active galaxy in its bare-AGN sample and analyzes XMM-Newton/Swift X-ray spectra, including continuum, soft-excess, and spectral-fitting behavior.

quotes.quote: 1H 0707--495 is a low redshift (z=0.04) narrow-line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) galaxy, which has extreme variability Turner1999, Boller2002 and spectral shape with a strong soft-excess and relativistic broad iron emission line Fabian2009 . This was the first AGN where an X-ray reverberation lag was detected Fabian2009 and this lag was shown to have a strong Fe line feature in the lag-energy spectrum Kara2013 . We adopted the black hole mass as 2 10^6 M _ from Zhau2005 . 1H 0707--495 was observed with XMM-Newton and Swift /XRT multiple times over a period of 20 years (Table table:obs_log ). We analysed these observations using a composite model which reads in XSPEC as, TBabs*zTBabs*(powerlaw+powerlaw+zGauss) .

sources.summary: The paper models XMM-Newton variability for 1H 0707--495, noting that the composite model matches its rms spectrum while a large spectral drop above 7 keV remains unexplained.

quotes.quote: We have calculated variability spectra from XMM-Newton data of NGC 4051 (2001 May 16/17 observation), MCG--6-30-15 (2001 August 2/3) and 1H 0707--495 (2002 October 13/14). We used background subtracted light curves with 256-s resolution, accumulated from all available instruments (PN only for NGC 4051, both MOS and PN for MCG--6-30-15 and 1H 0707--495).
Such a composite model of the variability can describe the rms spectra seen from MCG--6-30-15 and 1H 0707--495, as shown by solid curves in Figs. fig:3agn (a) and (b).

For example, 1H 0707--495 shows a large drop in the energy spectrum above 7 keV (e.g. Fabian et al. 2004), which cannot be explained by a highly ionized ( 3.5 ) absorber in our model. This feature requires a more complex model of the velocity structure than the simple Gaussian smearing assumed here, if it arises from the absorber.

sources.summary: The paper reports that 1H 0707-495 is best fit with absorption near 1 keV, favoring broad absorption or two absorption edges over an emission-line model.

quotes.quote: An absorption feature definitely gives the best fit in 1H 0707--495. A broad absorption line improves the fit significantly ( ^ 2 / =404/335 ), as per Table 4. An equally good ^ 2 can be obtained, but for 1 additional free parameter, using two edges instead of a single Gaussian; the first edge is at E= 1.09 0.03 keV with =0.63 0.27 and the second at E= 0.90 0.03 keV with =0.5 0.2 . Leighly (1997b) also find evidence for one or more absorption features near 1 keV. The addition of a Gaussian emission line in the range 0.6--3 keV, instead of an absorption feature, does not provide any improvement in the fit.

sources.summary: The paper uses 1H0707-495 as a close NLS1 comparison, emphasizing similar X-ray spectral features and broad Fe L/Fe K line improvements under a reflection interpretation.

quotes.quote: The X-ray spectrum of 1H0707-495 shows similar features at 1.3 keV and in the Fe K band.
Thus, we investigate here the consistency of the source emission with the reflection interpretation and its similarity with 1H0707-495.

This study is important to check the similarity with 1H0707-495. We observe that, as in the case of 1H0707-495, a significant improvement is achieved for all fits when two broad disc lines with energies of 1 keV and E>6 keV (depending on the continuum model) are added to the continuum A ^2 of 385 and 79 is observed when a broad Fe L and Fe K line is respectively added to the source spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1H0707-495 as a bright narrow-line Seyfert 1 with broad iron K and L lines, a Fe-L reverberation lag, sub-Eddington accretion, and an untruncated disk.

quotes.quote: For 1H0707-495, a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy (NLS1), broad iron K and L emission lines were recently found (Fabian et al. 2009). The bright Fe-L emission allows us to detect a reverberation lag of 30s between the direct X-ray continuum and its reflection from matter falling into the black hole, indicating that we obtain information from matter within a gravitational radius. The accretion rate is estimated to be (just) sub-Eddington. The observations point to an untruncated disk reaching inward to the ISCO and Comptonization of the irradiating X-rays by the disk (for a discussion of the very high state see Done, Gierli nski, and Kubota (2007)).

sources.summary: The paper uses 1H 0707-495 as the observed Seyfert benchmark, matching its iron-line emissivity profile and reverberation lags to a low, radially extended X-ray corona that later collapsed compactly.

quotes.quote: The emissivity profile of the accretion disc in the narrow line Seyfert 1 galaxy, 1H 0707-495 was determined by independently fitting the contributions to the relativistically broadened emission lines from successive radii in the disc wilkins_fabian_2011a . The accretion disc was found to have a steeply falling emissivity profile (with a power law index 7.8) in the inner region of the disc then flattening off to almost constant emissivity between radii of 5.6 \ and 34.5 \ before tending to a constant power law index of 3.3 (slightly steeper than the classical case) over the outermost parts of the disc, in broad agreement with previously predicted forms.

In 1H 0707-495, the emissivity profile obtained from the data appears to suggest an X-ray source as low as 2 \ above the plane of the accretion disc and extending outwards from the rotation axis to around 30 . Considering the emissivity profile of the accretion disc in terms of the properties of the primary X-ray source has also allowed the change in state of 1H 0707-495 observed in January 2011 (in which the spectrum was almost entirely reflection dominated with little direct continuum emission) to be interpreted in terms of the X-ray source collapsing to a confined region around the rotation axis, around 1.5-2 \ above the accretion disc.

sources.summary: The paper reviews prior reverberation modelling of 1H 0707-495 and finds its best-fitting spin differs significantly from unity, suggesting a non-rotating black hole.

quotes.quote: A first attempt at such a modelling of NXTLs was performed by chainakun12 for the AGN 1H 0707-495 using a GR light-bending model and a moving X-ray source in order to take account of the observed X-ray variability. Their conclusion was that a more complex physical model is required in order to explain both the source's geometry and intrinsic variability. More recently, wilkins13 using the full GR treatment with a variety of different geometries of corona, for the same AGN 1H 0707-495, found the the X-ray source extends radially outwards to around 35 \ and at a height of around 2 \ above the plane of the accretion disc and that propagating fluctuations might account for the positive low frequency time delays.
On the other hand, the best-fitting spin parameters differ significantly from unity in the case of NGC 4051, Ark 564, 1H 0707-495 and NGC 3516 suggesting that they may host a non-rotating BH.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1H 0707-495 as a Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 with a strong soft excess, archival XMM evidence favoring reflection, and a predicted NuSTAR diagnostic role.

quotes.quote: Two Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 nuclei with strong soft excesses have recently been studied in detail using XMM data: 1H 0707-495 and IRAS 13224-3809. and find that reflection can successfully fit the spectrum of 1H 0707-495; and find the same for IRAS 13224-3809. We use the archival XMM data to estimate S_ softex for both of these sources and find that they are 3.3 (1H 0707-495) and 2.6 (IRAS 13224-3809); therefore according to the scheme found in this paper, their soft excesses are both sufficiently strong to favour a reflection-dominated scenario. Broad-band observations with NuSTAR should be able to confirm this and locate both objects on the R-S_ softex plot.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes archival XMM-Newton data for 1H0707--495, describing strong broad iron spectral features, significant X-ray variability, and an 8000 s variation-amplitude drop near 7 keV.

quotes.quote: As for AGN, we have analyzed the data of 1H0707--495 ( M 10^6M_ ), which is known to have a strong ``broad iron spectral feature'' and exhibit significant X-ray time variations (see e.g. miz14 and references therein). We used the archival XMM-Newton data obtained in 2010 (ID=0653510301-601), the total exposure time of which is 399 ks. Figure 1H0707 shows the X-ray spectrum of 1H0707--495, and Figure 1H0707_DVF shows the variation amplitude at a timescale of 8000 s. In Figure 1H0707_DVF , we can clearly see that the variation amplitude drops at 7 keV, which is consistent with gal04 and fab04 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1H0707--495 as another Seyfert where the VPC model explains soft X-ray variation, with fewer absorber components than MCG-6-30-15.

quotes.quote: Mizumoto, Ebisawa Sameshima (2014) (MES2014 hereafter) has shown that the VPC model can also explain the soft X-ray spectral variations of the narrow Seyfert 1 galaxy 1H0707--405 observed with XMM and Suzaku below 10 keV. We have seen that the Variable Partial Covering (VPC) model, which has been proposed for MCG-6-30-15 (MEI2012) and 1H0707--495 (MES2014), can explain the 2--10 keV spectral variation of 20 other Seyfert galaxies observed with Suzaku.

1H0707--495 does not require the highly ionized absorber (Group C in Figure figABCD ; MES2014), and the 20 targets studied in this paper are grouped into four depending on the number and types of the absorbers required (Figure figABCD ). For 1H0707--495, the VPC model almost perfectly represents the flux variation in 0.5 -- 1 keV in timescales within day, while less satisfactorily in higher energies (MES2014); this also suggests the commonness of the independently variable hard component in Seyfert galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper uses 1H 0707 495 as the second NLSy1 QPO case, emphasizing its one-hour XMM-Newton period, intermittent detection, shared QPO frequency, and similar SMBH mass scale.

quotes.quote: The second QPO detection in a NLSy1 galaxy was more recently made by Pan et al. \ (2016) in 1H 0707 495; it again had a period of 1 hour (3800 s) and was seen in a XMM-Newton observation.

We note that our signal appears to be present throughout the full observation of MCG--06--30--15; this is in contrast to the QPOs detected in the other two NLSy1s, RE J1034 + 396 (Gierli n ski et al. \ 2008; Czerny et al. \ 2010) and 1H 0707 495 (Pan et al. \ 2016), in which the QPOs were only significant in portions of the data trains.

The frequency of this probable QPO in MCG--06--30--15 is nearly identical to those found in RE J1034 + 396 and 1H 0707 495 at 2.7 10^ 4 Hz. The best mass estimates of this QPO, at 3.26^ +1.59 _ 1.40 10^6 M_ or 1.6 0.4 10^6 M_ , are also very close to the others, which are 4^ +3 _ 1.5 10^6 M_ for RE J1034 + 396 (Zhou et al. \ 2010) and 5.2( 0.5 dex ) 10^6 M_ for 1H 0707 495 (Pan et al. \ 2016).

sources.summary: The appendix reports good UFO evidence in 1H 0707-495, with high-significance variability peaks, outflow velocities agreeing with prior studies, and a less clear pattern than IRAS 13224-3809.

quotes.quote: 1H 0707-495 has a similar spectral shape to IRAS 13324-3809 as they are both NLSy1 galaxies Hagino16 . Clear variability peaks which match count spectra absorption features with a high significance are present. The outflow velocity agrees, within its uncertainties, with past studies including Kosec19, Dauser12 . The pattern of peaks is less clear than in IRAS 13224-3809, which is likely due to the strong change in velocity of the absorption observed by Dauser12 .

sources.summary: The paper includes 1H 0707-495 among five AGN whose power spectra flatten with energy and are modeled with energy-dependent emissivity profiles.

quotes.quote: We apply strict data criteria (both in rejecting observations with an insufficient number of Fourier bins at frequencies below the white noise cutoff, and sources for which there were too few observations to provide error estimates), leaving us with five AGN: IRAS 13224-3809, 1H 0707-495, NGC 4051, ARK 564 and MRK 766. In all five cases, we observe that the power-law index -- which well describes the power spectrum in this frequency range (and is preferred over a broken power-law according to maximum likelihood fitting) -- is steep at low energies and becomes increasingly flat towards higher energies. Adopting the framework of 2006ArevaloUttley , we model the power-spectra of our AGN sample in five energy bands, assuming energy-dependent changes are driven by propagation only, and extract the emissivity index as a function of energy, parameterising it as a linear function. This modelling is remarkably successful in most cases, with the index starting at < 3 at low energies (which may well be consistent with an ADAF providing some of the soft excess emission, e.g. 2012DoneIntrinsicDiscEmission ) and rising to > 4 above 4-6 keV.

sources.summary: The paper models 1H 0707-495 reverberation lags, finding h_2 correlations and a softer vertically extended corona that leaves the disc less ionised.

quotes.quote: We find that for 1H 0707-495, h_2 is significantly correlated with _1 and anti-correlated with ionisation . Whilst the 1H 0707-495 corona extends upwards, the emission appears softer and the disc is less ionised.
We find the tendency of softer corona with increasing its vertical extent in both AGN. This produces a much less-ionised disc which is evident in 1H 0707-495.

sources.summary: The paper presents 1H 0707 495 as an NLS1 with enhanced hard-band short-timescale variability, similar Chandra behavior, and distinct but coupled soft-excess and power-law emission regions.

quotes.quote: In 1H 0707 495, the 2--10 keV SF is flatter toward short time scales than the SFs in the other bands, indicating more power on shortest time scales.

But in 1H 0707 495, several high-amplitude, short time-scale flares are seen in the hard band. It is worth noting that in the 2002 Chandra observation of 1H 0707 495, similar behavior was observed, except that the hard X-rays were much more variable on all time scales than the soft X-rays.
In 1H 0707 495, there is a soft excess and power law, and the difference in variability implies that they come from two physically distinct, although coupled, emission regions.

sources.summary: The paper cites 1H0707-495 as a Narrow Line Seyfert 1 whose broad Fe L-like feature and 30 sec lag support iron-line emission within a few gravitational radii.

quotes.quote: Recently Fabian et al. (2009) and Zoghbi et al. (2010) investigated the X-ray spectrum of the Narrow Line Seyfert 1 1H0707-495, showing a broad emission feature at 0.5-1.5 keV, with a profile nearly identical to the iron K emission line. This feature is attributed to relativistically broadened Fe L-shell emission lines. A time variability analysis of this feature with respect to the continuum emission revealed a time lag of 30 sec, strongly suggesting an origin of the iron lines within a few gravitational radii from the black hole.

sources.summary: The paper uses 1H0707-495 as the archetypal NLSy1 soft-lag source: its lag was interpreted as relativistic reverberation, and its break frequency anchors the simulations.

quotes.quote: The first significant detection of a soft/negative lag in AGN light curves was reported in the narrow line Seyfert 1 (NLSy1) galaxy 1H0707-495 (Fabian et al 2009, Zoghbi et al 2010), and interpreted as the signature of relativistic reflection which ``reverberates'' in response to continuum changes after a time equal to the light-crossing time from the source to the reflecting region.

The soft/negative lags detected in our analysis are perfectly consistent (in both amplitude and frequency) with those already reported in the literature for eight of the sources of our sample (Ark 564, Ar e valo et al 2006; 1H0707-495, Zoghbi et al 2010; Mrk 1040, Tripathi et al 2011; Mrk 766, MCG-6-30-15, Emmanoulopoulos et al 2011; RE J1034+396, Zoghbi Fabian 2011; NGC 3516, Turner et al 2011; PG 1211+143, De Marco et al 2011), even when slightly different energy bands are adopted.

The break frequency of each source has been estimated by rescaling the break frequency of 1H0707-495 (Zoghbi et al 2010) for the mass of the source, according to the scaling relationship provided by McHardy et al (2006), using the BH masses listed in Table tab:obs , and discarding the dependence on the mass accretion rate Assuming a linear scaling of the break frequency with the mass accretion rate (McHardy et al 2006), and using values tabulated in Ponti et al (2012) for the bolometric luminosity, this approximation introduces a negligible uncertainty, of a factor 2-3 , on the estimated break frequency.

sources.summary: The review presents 1H0707-495 as the first robust high-frequency soft-lag detection, identifying it with disc reverberation in a narrow-line Seyfert 1 with bright soft X-ray reflection.

quotes.quote: The first robust ( >5 ) detection of a short (30 s) soft, high-frequency lag was seen in a 500 ks long observation of 1H0707-495 and was identified as reverberation from the photoionised disc fabian09 . This source, a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy, has prominent, bright, reflection components in soft X-rays where the count rate is high, providing good signal-to-noise to detect the reverberation signal. The lag timescale is appropriate for the light crossing time of the inner disc of a few million M_ black hole.

sources.summary: The paper uses 1H 0707 495 as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 example requiring a radially extended low-height corona, soft X-ray continuum modeling, and a limited patchy coronal covering fraction.

quotes.quote: understanding_emis_paper , however, show that to simultaneously reproduce the steep inner emissivity profile and the flattening over the middle part of the accretion disc, the corona must be extended radially at a low height over the accretion disc to around 35 \ in the case of the narrow line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) galaxy 1H 0707 495 or to 10 \ in the NLS1 galaxy IRAS 13224 3809 iras_fix .

In order to produce even the softest X-ray continua seen in narrow line Seyfert 1 galaxies, for example 3 in 1H 0707 495, taking a lower bound coronal temperature T_ e =100 \ gives an optical depth _ e =0.44 and Compton y parameter y=0.34 .

Assuming the measured value of 1.8 for the NLS1 galaxy 1H 0707 495 with the spectral model of zoghbi+09 is a 10 per cent underestimate of this value due to the Comptonisation of the reflected photons, we can limit the covering fraction of the corona to between 25 and 50 per cent (we constrain the covering fraction only to lie within this broad range as the exact value will depend upon the exact extent geometry of the corona as well as the inclination of the accretion disc to the line of sight, which determines the number of reflected photons that are observed from the inner part of the accretion disc, while a broad range is illustrative of the coron \ of a number of NLS1 galaxies).

sources.summary: The paper uses 1H 0707-495 as the two-simultaneous-QPO AGN case; GRPM yields slow black-hole spin and circular or spherical QPO-source radii near 8.2-8.3.

quotes.quote: Later, 1H 0707-495 also showed the detection of a significant QPO at 3800 s and another at timescale 8265 s with relatively low significance in the X-ray light curve .

For the case having two simultaneous X-ray QPOs, 1H 0707-495 (see Table AGNXrayQPO ), we first solve the equations _ , a = _ 10 , _ pp , a = _ 20 (using Equations ( nuphicirc ) and ( nurcirc )), assuming M_ =5.2 10^6 M_ , as per GRPM to estimate the exact solution for ( , a ), which is found to be ( r_0=8.214 , a_0=0.0662 ).

Hence, we conclude that the spherical orbits, close to the black hole in the region, r_s= 8.214-8.323 with Q values between 0.0001-12.264 , are possible sources of the QPO frequencies observed in 1H 0707-495, while the most probable spin value to be a_p=0.139_ 0.139 ^ 0.183 with 2 confidence.

sources.summary: The paper discards all TESS sectors for 1H 0707 495 because nearby-pixel checks show a periodic flux-drop pattern caused by contamination from a nearby eclipsing binary.

quotes.quote: For example, the TESS light curves of 1H 0707 495 (Figure 1h0707-495tesslc ) display a periodic behavior where the flux drops regularly. However, this pattern is observed in most of the nearby pixels, and it is due to a contamination from a nearby eclipsing binary. All 6 TESS sectors are affected by the binary, so we rejected all the TESS light curves of this target for analysis.

sources.summary: The paper compares 1H 0707-495, a Narrow Line Seyfert 1 galaxy with a strong soft excess, against PG 1211+143 using several X-ray spectral models.

quotes.quote: We use the XMM-Newton data of the low redshift, optically bright quasar, PG 1211+143, and we compare it with the Narrow Line Seyfert 1 galaxy, 1H 0707-495, which has one of the strongest soft excesses seen.

The models are additionally modified by galactic absorption, cold absorption at the redshift of the source, and one (in 1H 0707-495) or two (in PG 1211+143) warm absorbers modeling the narrow absorption features. Thus, the only thing that differentiates the three models is the description of the origin of the soft excess. In PG 1211+143 the three models give virtually the same quality fits in terms of the reduced ^2 = 1.04 -1.06. In 1H 0707-495 the model of complex absorption results in a fit comparable to that of the model with a distinct spectral component (with reduced ^2 of 1.10 and 1.08, respectively).

sources.summary: The paper models 1H 0707-495 as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 whose sharp 7 keV X-ray drop can be fit by an outflowing-wind P Cygni profile.

quotes.quote: We demonstrate this explicitly by fitting the iron feature seen in data from 1H 0707-495 to a P Cygni profile.

For these data from 1H 0707-495 the apparent index in the 2-10 keV band (with an edge to describe the sharp drop at 7 keV) gives an extremely flat spectrum with 1.1 (Boller 2002), while the 0.3-1 keV band is apparently much steeper.
The section above shows that the sharp drop at 7 keV in the NLS1 1H 0707-495 can be fit by an unsaturated P Cygni line profile from an outflowing wind with total column of 5 10 ^ 24 cm ^ 2 i.e.

gmw_00000606.name: UGCA 92
gmw_00000606.overview: UGCA 92 is a nearby dwarf irregular galaxy located in the IC 342 group [arXiv:1203.1397]. It features a highly asymmetric distribution of ionized gas with multiple expanding shells [arXiv:1203.6213], and it may be interacting with the neighboring galaxy NGC 1569 [arXiv:1304.1484].

sources.summary: The paper derives UGCA 92's resolved-star formation history, finding an old dominant metal-poor episode, ongoing younger star formation, and no causal tie to NGC 1569's recent starburst.

quotes.quote: We present a quantitative star formation history of the nearby dwarf galaxy UGCA 92. This irregular dwarf is situated in the vicinity of the Local Group of galaxies in a zone of strong Galactic extinction (IC 342 group of galaxies). The galaxy was resolved into stars with HST/ACS including old red giant branch.

The main star formation activity period occurred about 8 -- 14 Gyr ago. About 84 per cent of the total stellar mass was formed during this event. There are also indications of recent star formation starting about 1.5 Gyr ago and continuing to the present.
Comparing our star formation history of UGCA 92 with that of NGC 1569 reveals no causal or temporal connection between recent star formation events in these two galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper reports strong ionized-gas asymmetry in UGCA 92, with shells and arcs near eastern star formation and multiple expanding shells of similar size and age.

quotes.quote: UGCA 92 We observe here the most significant asymmetry in the distribution of ionized gas relative to the centre among all the galaxies of our sample. In the line image, several shells and arcs are seen, most of which are related to the bright star formation region in the eastern part of the galaxy. There, five HII regions form a closed elongated loop in the centre of which a maximum in the distribution is observed. One can discern a broad inclined lane on the I- diagram (coloured green and orange), the corresponded points lying inside the above-mentioned loop of HII regions.

The observed features indicate that we are dealing with a shell (designated as UGCA 92 2), expanding at a rate of v_ exp = 1 2 V 30 , this value coincides with the maximal velocity dispersion in this region (see Tab.  tab_shells ). The velocity dispersion distribution easily reveals three more expanding shells of similar size and age. It is possible that the external arch structures in the western and eastern parts of the galaxy have the same nature, however, we failed to measure their expansion velocity.

sources.summary: UGCA 92 is treated as the nearby dwarf toward which NGC 1569's elongated cloud and v-shaped H I filaments extend, motivating an interaction scenario.

quotes.quote: Byrd Green Bank Telescope (GBT) was used to map a 9 x 2 region in \ line emission that includes NGC 1569 and IC 342 as well as two other dwarf galaxies. The primary objective for these observations was to search for structures potentially connecting NGC 1569 with IC 342 group members in order to trace previous interactions and thus, provide an explanation for the starburst and peculiar kinematics prevalent in NGC 1569. A large, half-degree diameter H 1 cloud was detected that shares the same position and velocity as NGC 1569. Also, two long structures were discovered that are reminiscent of intergalactic filaments extending out in a v-shaped manner from NGC 1569 toward UGCA 92, a nearby dwarf galaxy.

The GBT data also show v-shaped filaments extending a projected distance of 88 kpc in the direction of another dwarf UGCA 92. These observations paint a picture of a very disturbed dIm galaxy and prompt three possible explanations for the evolution that lead to the system seen today: 1) The extended H 1 features observed in the GBT map are primordial gas structures from cosmological filaments that are accreting onto NGC 1569. This gas falls into the inner disk thereby creating the disturbed kinematics and starburst. 2) NGC 1569 is a merger remnant of two gas rich dwarf galaxies. 3) NGC 1569 is interacting with UGCA 92 during which, the quiescent outer H 1 disk of NGC 1569 was disrupted and caused this gas to lose angular momentum and fall to the center.

gmw_00000607.name: UGCA 86
gmw_00000607.overview: UGCA 86 is an irregular dwarf galaxy located in the IC 342 group [arXiv:1304.1484, arXiv:2206.13311]. It is actively forming stars [arXiv:1002.2144] and features an extended neutral hydrogen distribution with a northern spur and a southern tail, likely resulting from a recent interaction with its spiral neighbor IC 342 [arXiv:1002.2144, arXiv:1304.1484].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGCA 86 as an actively star-forming dwarf with extended H I, a larger area than IC10, and clusters ranging from ancient metal-poor to young metal-rich and metal-poor objects.

quotes.quote: UGCA 86 This galaxy is actively forming stars, like IC10. However this takes place only in two optically bright regions, consistent with the maximum in HI distribution (Stil et al. 2005), which extends far from the optical boundaries of the galaxy. UGCA 86 covers a larger area than IC10 ( 3.8 2.7 kpc ^2 compared to 1.1 1.5 kpc ^2 ; see Karachentsev et al. 2004). The centre of the dwarf galaxy is at a distance of 331 kpc The de-projected spatial separation between UA86 and IC342 (in Mpc) was calculated as R^2 = D_ UA86 ^2 + D^2_ I342 2 D_ UA86 D^2_ I342 cos , where is the angular separation in degrees, and the distances to the galaxies are: D_ UA86 =2.96 Mpc, and D_ I342 =3.28 Mpc (Karachentsev et al. 2006, 2004, 2002). from the centre of its spiral neighbor IC342.

There is a number of clusters and cluster candidates in UGCA86 (see Tab. ua86 and also Georgiev et al. 2009). Spectra for four of them presented in this work suggest that two ancient clusters are metal-poor like the old clusters observed in IC10. We also found one young metal-rich cluster ( 20), and a young metal-poor one ( 13). The objects do not have any enhancement of elements relative to the solar value.

sources.summary: The paper describes UGCA 86 as separated from Milky Way confusion above 0 km/s, with a tenuous H I counter-tail, northern spur, and possible interaction with IC 342.

quotes.quote: These profiles show the extent of the Milky Way at velocities below 0 km s ^ 1 and show that IC 342 and UGCA 86 are well separated from Milky Way confusion above 0 km s ^ 1 . Also, there are no HVCs known that match the positions and velocities of IC 342 and UGCA 86 see e.g., bra99, loc02, loc03, sim06 . Therefore, it is assumed that all H 1 emission above 0 km s ^ 1 is associated with IC 342 and UGCA 86.

From these GBT data, an H 1 tail was detected extending from the south to the west from UGCA 86. sti05 identified an H 1 spur to the north of the galaxy in their high resolution H 1 data taken with the Dominion Radio Astrophysical Observatory (DRAO) Synthesis Telescope. They argued that the spur came from an interaction with IC 342, although this was difficult to prove partly because no counter tail was observed in their data.

UGCA 86 has a short, pronounced H 1 spur to the north of the disk, as discussed previously and shown in Figure fig:3 , which gives credence to the interaction being recent. Also, UGCA 86 is experiencing an episode of enhanced star formation but not a global starburst sti05 .

sources.summary: The paper uses UGCA 086 as a case study: an irregular IC 342 group galaxy where all 98 positionally matched NED and SIMBAD objects had matched classifications.

quotes.quote: Beginning with the 139 galaxies containing at least 1000 NED objects, we further selected the galaxy with the highest number of NED-SIMBAD matches that also had a classification match fraction of 99 (UGCA 086) and the galaxy with the lowest classification match fraction (NGC 2903). tab:CS_table summarizes the matching statistics for each of the case study galaxies. In both cases, the number of NED objects classified is an order of magnitude greater than the number of SIMBAD objects classified. UGCA 086 is an irregular galaxy at a distance of 3 Mpc, located in the IC 342 group kara20 ; it has substantial Galactic foreground extinction A_B = 4.06 mag and L_K = 10^ 9.1 L _ kara13 . All 98 of the NED and SIMBAD positionally-matched objects in this galaxy had matched classifications.

As fig:CS1_Object_Distribution shows, the positionally-matched objects are a small fraction of both the NED and SIMBAD contents for this galaxy: most of the 889 objects classified as `infrared source' by NED and most of the 448 objects classified as `star' by SIMBAD are not positionally matched to objects in the other catalogue. Figure fig:CS1_Match_Distribution shows the majority of object matches within the galaxy are strong matches regions identified by and included in both databases 1996AJ....112.1096M , while the few weak matches are largely forms of generalized matches such as `infrared source' (IR) to `galaxy' (G) or `ultraviolet Source' (UV) to `star' (*).

gmw_00000608.name: IC 342
gmw_00000608.overview: IC 342 is a nearly face-on, late-type spiral galaxy [arXiv:1502.05439, arXiv:1501.05586] that is weakly barred [arXiv:1312.4433] and hosts intense star formation in its core [arXiv:1808.05617]. It is the dominant member of the IC 342/Maffei group [arXiv:2405.13499] and may be interacting with UGCA 86 [arXiv:1304.1484].

sources.summary: The paper maps IC 342 radio polarization, finding a diffuse disk, cosmic-ray electron propagation, spiral-arm magnetic structures, outer density-wave effects, and a slow, weak mean-field dynamo.

quotes.quote: The strength and structure of interstellar magnetic fields and their relation to spiral arms in gas and dust are investigated in the nearby and almost face-on spiral galaxy IC 342. The total and polarized radio continuum emission of IC 342 was observed with high spatial resolution in four wavelength bands with the Effelsberg and VLA telescopes.

IC 342 hosts a diffuse radio disk with an intensity that decreases exponentially with increasing radius. The frequency dependence of the scalelength of synchrotron emission indicates energy-dependent propagation of the cosmic-ray electrons, probably via the streaming instability. The equipartition strength of the total field in the main spiral arms is typically 15 , that of the ordered field about 5 .

Fast MHD density waves can generate coincident spiral arms in gas and magnetic fields in the outer parts of IC 342. Magnetic armsar e offset from the spiral pattern in gas and dust; their generation and development by mean-field dynamo action probably need a spiral pattern that is stable over a few galactic rotation periods, which is probably the case for the galaxy NGC 6946. The mean-field dynamo in IC 342 is slow and weak, probably disturbed by the bar, tidal interaction, or a transient spiral pattern.

sources.summary: The paper reports optical SNR candidates in the nearby spiral galaxy IC342, giving candidate coordinates, sizes and line fluxes and estimating their contribution to the galaxy H emission.

quotes.quote: We present the detection of 16 optical supernova remnant (SNR) candidates in the nearby spiral galaxy IC342. The candidates were detected by applying [ S ii ] /H ratio criterion on observations made with the 2 m RCC telescope at Rozhen National Astronomical Observatory in Bulgaria. In this paper, we report the coordinates, diameters, H and [ S ii ] fluxes for 16 SNRs detected in two fields of view in the IC342 galaxy. Also, we estimate that the contamination of total H flux from SNRs in the observed portion of IC342 is 1.4 .

In this paper, we present the second part of our study on the search for emission nebulae in the IC342 galaxy. In the first paper (Vu eti et al. 2013, hereafter Paper I) we presented the detection of 203 H ii regions in the observed portion of this spiral galaxy. That raised the number of known H ii regions 2.5 times in this part of IC342. In this paper, we give the details of the detection of 16 supernova remnant (SNR) candidates, out of which all except one represent SNR candidates detected for the first time.

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 342 as a nearly face-on spiral with strong core star formation, difficult soft X-ray constraints, extensive X-ray observations, and two luminous ULXs requiring broadband study.

quotes.quote: IC 342 is a nearly face-on spiral galaxy with intense star formation activity in its core becklin03-80 . A member of the IC 342/Maffei group of galaxies, it is located near the Galactic plane at b 10 , making it difficult to constrain X-ray emission 1 keV. IC 342 has been studied by all major X-ray observatories over the past four decades. \ high-resolution camera imaging (HRC-I) of the central 30 \ by 30 \ region by mak10-08 resulted in the detection of 23 sources. They found that one of the three historical ULXs detected by \ was actually coincident with the nuclear center and thus not a ULX.

rana02-15 recently used two epochs of / \ observations to investigate the two ULXs in IC 342. They found luminosities of 10^ 40 and 7.4 10^ 39 \ for sources X-1 and X-2, respectively. Joint spectral fitting of each source ruled out the possibility of a BH binary in a low/hard accretion state. They concluded that further broadband spectral studies are needed to identify the origin of the spectral components.

sources.summary: The paper compares simulated Faraday-depth reversals with IC342, noting the galaxy has low star formation and unclear spiral arms, and that simulated azimuthal FD trends resemble IC342 observations.

quotes.quote: Comparison with IC342 The FD reversal within the spiral arms are found in the galaxy IC342 which shows a low star formation rate and unclear spiral arms bec2015 . To compare to the observations, we made a plot of the FD in the spiral arms (see Fig. fig2 b). The periodical reversals of FD are observed in the spiral arms. Because the scale length correlates with the disk scale height, the reversal length of the FD signs are proportional to the radius. It means that these reversals are caused by the Parker instability.

These results support to the observational results for IC342 and theoretical predictions of the Parker instability. Fig. fig5 b shows the relation between azimuthal angle and FD inside 4 kpc < < 7 kpc for = 25 , whose inclination angle seems to be IC342. The tendency of azimuthal angle - FD relation shows similar with IC342.

sources.summary: The paper maps IC 342's inner molecular gas with GBT/Argus, finding differing tracer morphologies, a tight HCN-to-line ratio across the bar, and scale-dependent star-formation correlations.

quotes.quote: We report observations of the ground state transitions of , , , HCN, and \ at 88--115 GHz in the black inner region of the nearby galaxy IC 342. These data were obtained with the 16-pixel spectroscopic focal plane array Argus on the 100-m Robert C. Byrd Green Bank Telescope (GBT) at 6--9 \ resolution. In the nuclear bar region, the intensity distributions of (1--0) and (1--0) emission trace moderate densities, and differ from the dense gas distributions sampled in (1--0), HCN(1--0), and (1--0). We observe a constant HCN(1--0)-to- (1--0) ratio of 1.2 0.1 across the whole 1 kpc bar. This indicates that HCN(1--0) and (1--0) lines have intermediate optical depth, and that the corresponding \ of the gas producing the emission is of 10 ^ 4.5-6 .

IC 342 is a nearly face-on, spiral galaxy at 3.3 Mpc saha02 harboring starburst activity in its center. Its large angular size 20 ^ in optical and 80 ^ in HI, crosthwaite00 is well suited to detailed imaging with the GBT's beam sizes, while the concentration of molecular gas in the galactic center gives rich line emission e.g., downes92, turner92, meier00 .
Finally, we find a breakdown of the L_ IR L_ HCN correlation at high spatial resolution due to the effect of incomplete sampling of star-forming regions by HCN emission in IC 342.

sources.summary: The paper measures IC 342 with optical and infrared TRGB data, derives reddening from its main sequence, and reports a 3.45 Mpc distance consistent with its group.

quotes.quote: IC 342 The spiral galaxy IC 342 suffers the least extinction among the three dominant galaxies in the Maffei/IC342 group. It was discovered early in the 1890's dre95 due to its bright visual magnitude V = 8.3 , but99 . For IC 342, where the TRGB is detected at both optical and IR bands, we derive its reddening by comparing the color of the main sequence to those of other spiral galaxies with small reddening.

From 7 members of the IC 342 group, kar03 reported that the mean distance toward the IC 342 group is 3.28 0.15 Mpc. From F814W TRGB distances toward these galaxies in the EDD, which are derived from the calibration of riz07 , the mean distance toward the IC 342 group is 3.25^ +0.17 _ 0.24 Mpc. The mean group distance is also in agreement with the distance of IC 342.

With the IR TRGB method, new distance estimates from the F110W TRGB are 3.45 ^ +0.13 _ 0.13 Mpc for IC 342 (after averaging the F814W TRGB distance), 3.43 ^ +0.32 _ 0.23 Mpc for Maffei 1 and 3.52 ^ +0.32 _ 0.30 Mpc for Maffei 2.

sources.summary: The review emphasizes IC 342 as a nearby spiral with an unusually large synchrotron scale length, fast convective cosmic-ray transport, rudimentary magnetic arms, and weak large-scale regular fields.

quotes.quote: The nearby galaxy IC 342 (Fig. fig:ic342 ) reveals an exceptionally large synchrotron scale length of about 16 kpc beck15 . The radial scale length and its frequency dependence give us a general idea about the speed and type of CRE propagation. At wavelengths of 1 m, synchrotron and inverse Compton losses are weaker than at shorter wavelengths, so that CREs can diffuse further outwards from their locations of origin. This is clearly seen in M 31 and M 33 berkhuijsen13 .
